Categories:
- basic_research: Understanding mechanisms, pathways, disease processes (knowledge output)
- biotools: Developing research tools, assays, methods, platforms (tool output)
- therapeutics: Developing drugs, treatments, therapies (treatment output)
- diagnostics: Developing clinical tests, biomarker panels (diagnostic test output)
- medical_device: Developing physical devices for patients (device output)
- digital_health: Patient-facing software, apps, clinical decision support
- other: Health services, behavioral interventions, epidemiology

application_id: 10948694
activity_code: K23
title: Sleep Health and Cognitive Function among Middle-aged Adults: the SleepCog Study
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cognitive impairment (CI), including Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD), are prevalent in later life with limited treatment options. Sleep disturbance (SD) is a significant risk factor for the development of CI/ADRD in older adults. This project seeks to examine how specific types of SD may be associated with poorer cognitive function and worse health outcomes earlier - among younger, middle-aged adults. We will develop and pilot test an efficient, scalable, primary care strategy for the routine detection of SD to mitigate CI/ADRD risk.

application_id: 10948915
activity_code: F31
title: The role of dietary cholesterol in Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease through the action of gut microbiota
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) and Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) are associated with gut dysbiosis and high dietary cholesterol intake and they affect a quarter of the world population, yet there are no FDA-approved treatments. This proposal aims to determine the mechanistic contribution of dietary cholesterol to NAFLD/NASH through actions of gut microbiota. Uncovering this aspect of NAFLD/NASH pathophysiology may lead to implementation of dietary and/or probiotic treatments to alleviate the burden of NAFLD/NASH worldwide.

application_id: 10948991
activity_code: K99
title: Fast High-Resolution Microstructure Diffusion MRI Exploiting Data Redundancy
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Diffusion magnetic resonance imaging (dMRI) plays a crucial role in routine clinical neuroimaging and has shown potential in new applications ranging from detecting microstructural lesions to guiding neurosurgery. This project addresses the need to improve the performance of dMRI by enabling high-resolution microstructure imaging with fast acquisition times, allowing clinical translation of impactful research applications. Ultimately, the proposed approach has the potential to enhance the quality of care for individuals with neurological disorders.

application_id: 10765630
activity_code: F31
title: Machine learning methods for the analysis and modeling of spatial proteomics data
org_name: CARNEGIE-MELLON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Recent advances in multiplexed imaging have led to a new generation of technologies for the spatial profiling of the proteome at a single-cell resolution (spatial proteomics), which promise to revolutionize our understanding of cell-cell interactions in the human body. This project aims to develop computational methods to use spatial proteomics data for building a global human tissue atlas. Incorporating spatial information into our understanding of interactions at a single-cell level will reshape how we view development, aging, disease, and more.

Continuous blood sugar monitoring has revolutionized type 1 diabetes care but remains underutilized to sustainably support exercise and related behaviors. application_id: 10949209
activity_code: K99
title: Role of Anterior Cingulate Cortex Ensemble Regulation of Spinal Cord Excitability in Associative Opioid Analgesic Tolerance.
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Opioids are important pain medications, but chronic opioid use in a particular environment can produce tolerance that is associated with that environment. However, when opioids are consumed in a different place, tolerance can be reversed rapidly, which can lead to fatal overdose. This proposal will explore the contributions of brain and spinal circuits to this environmental control of analgesic tolerance to help inform the development of new treatments aimed at decreasing pain and opioid overdose.

application_id: 10912567
activity_code: K08
title: Defining the antigenic determinants of the adaptive immune response in IgG4-related disease
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative IgG4-related disease is an autoimmune fibrotic disorder that affects multiple organs and causes tumor-like masses, often misdiagnosed as metastatic cancer. Thus far, we have determined which types of immune cells are likely drivers of the disease, but the proteins driving this response and whether they are self or foreign, remain largely unknown. The knowledge gained from this research is hoped to lead to a more in-depth ability to learn how immune cells interact with each other in causing fibrosis.

activity_code: K99
title: Novel gene regulatory networks underlying two specialized modes of craniofacial development in a new model system of pupfishes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Project Narrative The spatial and temporal coordination of gene transcription is crucial to ensure that fundamental biological processes, such as embryonic development, are successfully orchestrated, thereby allowing for the normal functioning of an organism. By leveraging an endemic group of pupfishes from the Bahamas, which exhibit extremely divergent craniofacial morphologies, I propose a multi-omics approach to unravel and functionally test the gene networks and regulatory regions of the genome controlling changes in gene expression underlying their naturally divergent craniofacial development.

application_id: 10949606
activity_code: K99
title: Fcy Receptor-Mediated Pharmacogenomics of Antibody Therapies in Type 1 Diabetes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Antibody and antibody fusion protein therapies may halt loss of endogenous insulin production in recently- diagnosed Type 1 Diabetes (T1D) through Fc gamma receptor (FcγR)-associated mechanisms. The goals of this proposal are: 1) to identify genetic variants in FcγR loci that associate with therapeutic efficacy in completed T1D immunotherapy trials and 2) to evaluate the impact of associated loci on FcγR protein expression and function. These studies are expected to aid in generation of a precision medicine strategy to target subjects with or at-risk for T1D who may benefit from immunotherapies with FcγR-associated mechanisms of action.

org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA

application_id: 10840797
activity_code: K24
title: Mentoring and Patient-Oriented Research on Social Exposures and CVD Risk in Underrepresented Women
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE African-American women have strikingly high rates of cardiovascular disease (CVD) morbidity and mortality, and this is especially true at midlife. Theoretical and non-human primate models suggest that social stressors and ovarian aging may play a role, and the science proposed in this application is designed to examine these associations in a cohort of African-American women. The mentoring and training proposed in this application is designed to foster a supportive environment for the careers of junior patient-oriented researchers interested in social exposures and CVD risk in vulnerable groups of women.

activity_code: DP2
title: The evolutionary landscape of HIV broadly neutralizing antibody development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Broadly neutralizing antibodies are a necessary component of an effective HIV vaccine, but an incomplete understanding of broadly neutralizing antibody development has impeded attempts to elicit these desirable antibody responses via vaccination. Here, I use high-throughput mutational scanning techniques to elucidate the molecular evolutionary forces and constraints that guide HIV antibody development. These detailed mutational maps will enable the design of improved HIV vaccine immunogens and therapeutic antibodies and reveal the general principles of broadly neutralizing antibody function and development with relevance for various pathogens where broad antibody responses are necessary for pathogen control.

title: Selective neuronal autophagy in phosphorylated tau degradation and Alzheimer's disease
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) pathology is defined by aberrant protein aggregation within the brain, underscoring the
importance of neuronal protein degradation and the autophagy-lysosomal system. Of particular interest are the
selective autophagy proteins optineurin (OPTN) and BCL-2-associated athanogene 3 (BAG3), which appear to
be relevant in the degradation of neuronal tau protein. Therefore, this proposal aims to further the understanding
of tau protein degradation via autophagy and define the molecular mechanisms governing OPTN and BAG3
regulation and function in neurons, which may lead to novel therapeutic modalities.

activity_code: F32
title: Linking neuronal identity transcription factors to neural circuit establishment and maintenance
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OREGON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Neural circuits generate sensation, movement and cognition, and aberrant neural circuits are implicated in many neurodevelopmental and psychiatric disorders. Thus, is it important to identify the developmental mechanisms that establish neuronal connectivity, including transcription factors, and determine whether they also maintain neural circuits into adulthood. Drosophila is a simple and powerful system to address these questions, with the goal of identifying mechanisms conserved from fly to mammals.

application_id: 10920474
activity_code: K23
title: Does social motivation in adolescence differentially predict the impact of childhood threat exposure on developing suicidal thoughts and behaviors
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Childhood threat exposure (e.g., family/community violence) is a key risk factor for developing suicidal thoughts and behaviors; yet, not all adolescents with childhood threat exposure develop these symptoms. Disruptions to social motivation – the motivations driving the desire and effort to socially interact – may play a role in differentiating suicide risk. The current study will identify whether maladaptive social motivation presentations during adolescence moderate the link between childhood threat exposure and suicidal thoughts/behaviors in an effort to improve risk identification and mechanistic targets for interventions.

title: Clonal Hematopoiesis in a Biracial Population

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed research seeks to study the factors associated with development of clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP), and racial disparities in associated outcomes, in order to identify individuals at increased risk. title: Harnessing SHIVs and Sequential Immunization to Elicit V3 Glycan-Targeted bNAbs against HIV-1
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There are over 37 million people currently living with HIV and an additional 1.8 million are newly infected each year, yet despite decades of research we still do not have an efficacious vaccine. application_id: 10922695
activity_code: K01
title: COMputerized PAtient-centered Collaborative Technology (COMPACT) to Support Personalized Decision Making in Breast Cancer
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE At least half of the more than a quarter million newly diagnosed breast cancer patients do not make treatment decisions that are optimally aligned with their values and preferences, resulting in decisional regret and poorer treatment experiences and outcomes. We will develop a COMputerized PAtient-centered Collaborative Technology (COMPACT) to support personalized treatment decision making in breast cancer. Better personalization and involvement of patients in decision making can improve patient outcomes, reduce healthcare costs, and may improve health equity by supporting consideration of socioeconomic and other factors (e.g., values, goals) that influence treatment decisions.

application_id: 10924057
activity_code: K26
title: Mentorship of Individuals from Historically Under-Represented Groups in Health Sciences Research
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: The proposed project will provide a mentorship to predoctoral and postdoctoral scholars from diverse backgrounds who are conducting research within the mission of NIDDK. The PI will provide theory-driven and evidence-based interventions that target scientific self-efficacy and identity to increase perseverance to achieve a career as a researcher. title: APP as a mediator of amyloid beta effects on CaMKII synaptic functions
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alzheimer’s disease is a severely debilitating neurodegenerative disease, marked in early stages by synaptic deficits thought to be caused by the amyloid-β peptide. application_id: 10902602
activity_code: F32
title: Discovery of the mechanisms of resistance for the anthelmintic emodepside
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Parasitic nematodes are the most abundant, diverse, and destructive macroparasites on the planet, placing heavy health burdens on billions of people in developing countries and causing billions of dollars lost annually in crops and livestock. This proposal seeks to identify genes, transcripts, and small molecules involved in emodepside resistance, a promising new anthelmintic with completely uncharacterized resistance mechanisms. Completion of this proposal will identify emodepside resistance genes and establish biochemical mechanisms behind the metabolism of emodepside, which are both needed to implement targeted and effective anthelmintic treatment plans.

application_id: 10821656
activity_code: F31
title: Host-Pathogen Interactions in a Cystic Fibrosis Polymicrobial Infection Model
org_name: DARTMOUTH COLLEGE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cystic fibrosis (CF) affects more than 100,000 people worldwide and causes the formation of thick mucus in the lungs, leading to the chronic infection of various pathogens that are prone to developing antibiotic tolerance. This application proposes that preexposure to bacterial extracellular vesicles leads to epigenetic changes in host bronchial epithelial cells that alter the host immune response, contributing to the establishment of chronic lung infections. The project will serve as a foundation for the candidate to develop skills as an academic researcher and could lead to the development of novel therapeutic treatments for CF.

title: Investigating KCNQ1 Mistrafficking in Long QT Syndrome
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative Loss of function mutations in the voltage gated potassium channel KCNQ1 cause type 1 long QT syndrome (LQT1), a cardiac disorder associated with arrythmias, cardiac arrest, and sudden unexpected death. Previous studies suggest that decreased efficiency of trafficking to the plasma membrane (mistrafficking) may be a common mechanism of KCNQ1 loss of function in LQT1. In the proposed project, I plan to investigate the role of KCNQ1 mistrafficking in LQT1 by classifying the trafficking phenotype of all possible KCNQ1 variants and determine whether KCNQ1 trafficking can be altered with small molecules by performing a high throughput screen for compounds that modify the trafficking of KCNQ1.

title: Molecular Mechanisms Underlying Endothelial Weibel-Palade Body Biogenesis and Exocytosis
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative von Willebrand factor (vWF) is essential for normal hemostasis and thrombosis and is involved in other important processes such as angiogenesis and cancer cell metastasis. Regulated release of vWF depends on normal maturation of its storage granule, the Weibel-Palade bodies (WPBs), and subsequent fusion of WPBs to the plasma membrane, processes which are poorly understood. Using live cell microscopy, pulldown of endogenously tagged proteins, and a genetically modified mouse model, we propose to elucidate the endosomal pathway in WPB biogenesis, the associated trafficking machinery, and regulation of WPB exocytosis by the exocyst complex.

title: De-Implementation of Low Value Imaging in Pulmonary Embolism (DELVE PE)
org_name: BAYSTATE MEDICAL CENTER, INC.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Computed tomographic pulmonary angiograms (CTPA) for pulmonary embolism (PE) are ordered in nearly 2% of all 120 million Emergency Department visits in the United States each year; however, many of these are avoidable. Despite prior research in this area, unnecessary testing remains common and subjects patients to additional costs, ionizing radiation, risk of anaphylaxis from contrast dye, and harms from overdiagnosis and overtreatment. To improve the quality and efficiency of care for patients with suspected PE, this investigation will refine, pilot, and evaluate the acceptability and appropriateness of an empirically-derived multi-dimensional strategy that targets unique barriers to de-implementing low- value imaging.

activity_code: K08
title: Regulation of signaling pathways mediating epithelial cell injury in the neonatal intestine
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC) is a severe and often fatal intestinal disease of prematurity that culminates in irreversible intestinal epithelial cell injury. title: The function and evolution of female-expressed seminal fluid proteins
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS

application_id: 10974016
activity_code: F31
title: Determining the function of TRPC6 channels in a subpopulation of VTA dopamine neurons
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative: Neurons in the ventral tegmental area (VTA) that express the neuropeptide, tachykinin receptor 3 (Tacr3), are a distinct subpopulation of dopamine-producing neurons that promote reward reinforcement behavior. However, the intracellular signaling pathways that act downstream of this Gq-protein coupled receptor to regulate this behavior are unresolved. This proposal will investigate the role of transient receptor potential canonical type 6 channels in regulating the physiology and function of VTA-Tacr3 neurons, which will provide important insights into the therapeutic potential of this understudied ion channel in the central nervous system.

application_id: 10892976
activity_code: K23
title: Clinical and biologic risk factors for post-tuberculosis lung disease (PTLD) in people with and without HIV
org_name: TULANE UNIVERSITY OF LOUISIANA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Post-tuberculosis lung disease (PTLD), which develops in up to half of people with pulmonary tuberculosis, is an increasingly recognized cause of lung disease with up to 4.3 million new cases worldwide every year. However, the underlying biologic mechanisms that lead to PTLD are still poorly understood. This proposal aims to understand the underlying clinical host factors and host immunological factors that lead to PTLD, measuring lung function over two-years after pulmonary tuberculosis and using state-of-the art techniques including RNA sequencing.

application_id: 10904673
activity_code: F31
title: Manganese dioxide as a nanozyme to mitigate oxidative stress in osteoarthritis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Osteoarthritis (OA), the leading cause of disability in the United States, has no effective therapy to slow or prevent disease progression. Oxidative stress plays a central role in the development of OA, however attempts to mitigate oxidative stress with antioxidants have been limited by poor bioavailability and limited stability within the joint. Thus, the objective of this proposal is to utilize a cartilage-targeting nanomaterial with antioxidant-like properties that overcomes limitations of conventional therapies to effectively mitigate oxidative stress, thereby reducing damage to joint structure and preserving joint function in post traumatic OA.

org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Influenza A virus (IAV) infection and pneumonia represent the leading infectious causes of death in the United States, and disproportionately affect the elderly. title: The Transmission Biology of Mycobacterium Tuberculosis
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Transmission of Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb), the causative agent of tuberculosis, requires that the
organism survive aerosolization. This proposal exposes Mtb to air drying stress and tests the hypothesis that
drying induces an adaptive metabolic response. The identification and inhibition of bacterially-encoded
determinants of transmission would be a novel approach to halting the spread of disease.

activity_code: K01
title: The Neural Basis of Active Sensory Gain Suppression in the Somatosensory System
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Object sensing and manipulation require selective focus of tactile sensory signals from specific locations on the hand. Here, we determine how selective attention implements mechanisms of gain suppression to filter out irrelevant tactile information, and enhance sensory representations encoding attended cutaneous stimuli. Results from this study will provide fundamental insights into how distracting sensory information is discarded in brain, which may lead to a better understanding of the underlying circuits mediating tactile perceptual deficits in Autism and Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder populations. Our studies will also have a direct translational impact on Brain-Machine-Interface (BMI) solutions that are specific to neuroprosthetic applications.

application_id: 10994395
activity_code: F30
title: Deconvoluting the contributions of the IFNy pathway components to lung TME and tumor immunity
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative: Although immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) therapeutics have proved the possible efficacy of immunotherapies in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), most patients do not respond to these treatments durably. application_id: 10996333
activity_code: F30
title: Evaluating the impact of malaria on immunogenicity of the Ebola virus vaccine
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative Studies from the Zaire Ebola virus (EBOV) outbreak in West Africa suggested that the FDA-approved EBOV vaccine can be highly effective; however, more recent data from the Democratic Republic of the Congo revealed that EBOV vaccine failure rates may be as high as 30%. We postulate that acute Plasmodium parasitemia that is prevalent in Central Africa decreases immune responses to the FDA-approved vaccine. Here, we use a mouse model to mechanistically define the effect of acute Plasmodium infection on the efficacy of EBOV vaccine.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Among those who initiate use of alcohol and other substances in early- to mid-adolescence, lifetime rates of alcohol and drug use disorders are 1.3 to 3 times the national average. activity_code: F32
title: Caregiver influences on item-context memory and arousal in toddlers
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIV NEW YORK MORNINGSIDE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Memories from early childhood do not persist into adulthood – a phenomenon that remains a neurobiological puzzle. This proposal aims to examine item-context memory and arousal in toddlers, and how it is modulated by caregivers, in order to advance understanding of the functions and plasticity of developing learning and memory systems in toddlers. In the long-term, this work has implications for why infant/toddler memories are so fragile and fleeting, yet at the same time, early caregiving experiences have enduring effects on cognition and socioemotional outcomes.

activity_code: F31
title: Elucidating RNA determinants for reovirus packaging
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE To become infectious, viruses with multi-segmented double-stranded RNA genomes need to identify and package the correct collection of RNA molecules, but how this process occurs is poorly understood. The studies proposed in this application will increase our understanding of selective RNA packaging mechanisms for reovirus, a segmented double-stranded RNA virus that is currently in clinical trials as a cancer therapy. These discoveries could aid in our future ability to engineer reoviruses that are more efficient in targeting cancer cells.

title: Assembly and Disassembly Processes in the HIV-1 Life Cycle
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE HIV-1 undergoes multiple morphological transformations during its life cycle including assembly of the immature virion, maturation of the viral core, and uncoating among many others. Due to the necessity of each of these transitions for viral infectivity, drugs that inhibit these processes or lead to off-pathway structures have great potential as antiretroviral therapies. In this work, a series of multi-scale computational investigations, in conjunction with leading experimentalists, will unveil the molecular mechanisms involved in these morphological transformations.

title: Vocabulary Encoding and Retention in Autism via Social Cognition (VERAS)
org_name: NORTHEASTERN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Over half of all autistic individuals demonstrate clinical levels of language impairment, including in vocabulary. Vocabulary skills correlate highly with vocational independence, mental health, and quality of life for autistic individuals – especially adults – and are the sole linguistic area to remain flexible across the lifespan. By studying how theory of mind difficulties in autistic individuals may be inhibiting vocabulary growth and determining the degree to which the underlying compensatory routes or alternative contexts may be leveraged in intervention, we will open the door to further linguistic interventions that can improve vocabulary – and thus language – outcomes even into adulthood and, through this, increase lifelong success.

activity_code: F32
title: Physiologically-inspired exoskeleton controller to enhance human balance
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Robotic exoskeletons improve an individual’s ability to navigate their community and have the potential to reduce the risk of falling; however, current exoskeletons do not assist in balance control. This project will develop a generalizable, physiologically-inspired controller for a multi-joint exoskeleton to enhance reactive balance control during standing and walking. This work could lead to significant advances in the usability of robotic exoskeletons for balance control, which can ultimately be used to develop exoskeletons aimed at improving balance in individuals at a high risk for falls, including older adults and individuals with neuromuscular pathologies.

title: Biofilm antigens drive natural immunity to Streptococcus pneumoniae.

title: Deciphering the role of the vaginal microbiome in spontaneous preterm birth via consideration of host genetics
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Narrative
Spontaneous preterm birth (sPTB) poses an urgent, global threat to both maternal and neonatal health.
Though the vaginal microbiome and human genetics have each been independently associated with
prematurity, research has yet to disentangle the tripartite relationship between all three entities. I will analyze
paired human genotype-microbiome data to infer causality of microbes on sPTB and to predict sPTB risk from
both microbes and genetic variants.

activity_code: F31
title: A Mechanoimmunological Basis for Metastatic Site Preference
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative Metastases form in a wide range of target organs that vary not only in their chemical composition but also in their biophysical properties. We have found that patrolling immune cells preferentially engage and selectively destroy stiffer cancer cells and that cancer cells mimic the stiffness of their environment. Our proposed studies will investigate if and how the rigidity of the metastatic microenvironment influences the stiffness dependent killing of cancer cells and, by extension, whether the interplay between tissue mechanics and immune function effectively dictates where metastasis grows, a process otherwise known as metastatic site preference.

title: Identifying the Role of Fibroblast-Macrophage Crosstalk in Aortic Valve Stenosis Sexual Dimorphism
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Aortic valve stenosis affects ~13% of the US population over 75 years of age and interestingly men have a two- fold higher risk for developing calcified valves whereas women tend to present with severely fibrotic valves. This proposal aims to investigate these sex differences, particularly the dynamic interplay between sex-specific immune cell (macrophage) interactions with resident valve interstitial cells (VICs) and how they lead to aortic valve stenosis sexual dimorphism. Our approach includes the 3D culture of VICs and macrophages in hydrogel matrices to identify the effects of secreted factors and direct contact on their phenotype and function, toward revealing targets for sex-specific, non-surgical (e.g., pharmaceutical) therapies.

title: Cocaine effects on spatial patterns of striatal dopamine release
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cocaine use disorder (CUD) is defined by a variety of symptoms, including disruptions in non-drug stimulus processing. title: Fascin1 in Growth Cone Motility and Guidance
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Proper wiring of the brain’s neuronal circuits is critical for development and a key step in this process is axon guidance, during which the highly motile tip of axons, called a growth cone, can sense and respond to extracellular guidance cues to find target cells. Errors in axon guidance as well as irregularities in Fascin1 expression levels have both been linked to epilepsy.

application_id: 10983476
activity_code: K08
title: Impact of Aiolos on intestinal intraepithelial lymphocytes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Narrative Pathological activation of intestinal intraepithelial lymphocytes (IELs) is implicated in exacerbation of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), but the mechanisms that suppresses IEL activation during intestinal inflammation remain unknown. I discovered the novel regulatory roles of Aiolos, a transcription factor encoded by Ikzf3, in IEL activation, and this project will investigate how Aiolos-deficient IELs modulate host responses to gut inflammation. title: Shear stress-activated synthetic cells for targeted drug release in stenotic blood vessels
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Constriction of arterial blood vessels due to atherosclerosis is a leading cause of death worldwide, but current treatment is limited because administering clot-lysing drug entails a high risk of bleeding when distributed throughout the body. Using the fact that stenotic vessels exhibit abnormally high shear stress, we propose to develop shear stress-responsive synthetic cells as a targeted drug delivery system that can selectively release drugs in constricted blood vessels. This work will allow a deeper understanding of a mechanosensitive protein employed in this system to enable its broader use in the future and will contribute to expanding the application boundary of the synthetic cell in the biomedicine field.

title: Identifying effective and efficient approaches to tuberculosis screening in Brazilianprisons
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: Project Narrative. Increasing tuberculosis case detection, especially in high-risk groups, is critical to meet the World Health Organization’s End TB Strategy to reduce tuberculosis incidence by 90% globally by 2035. A significant barrier to achieving this goal is that a large proportion of patients are unable to produce sputum and are therefore are not evaluated with commonly used and recommended diagnostics such as GeneXpert, culture, and smear testing. activity_code: K23
title: teleABLE: Adapting a Behavioral Activation-Based Intervention to Reduce Post-Stroke Sedentary Behavior Using Telehealth
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Elevated levels of sedentary behavior are prominent and persistent among stroke survivors and associated with poor cardiovascular and cardiometabolic health outcomes which elevate the risk for recurrent stroke. application_id: 11057048
activity_code: K01
title: Novel Mechanisms Of MicroRNA-Mediated Anabolic Effects In Age-Related Osteoarthritis
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative We discovered a robust anabolic response in human cartilage that follows a distal-proximal axis gradient (high in the ankle joint and low in the hip joint); this response was modulated by the same master regulators that regulate limb regeneration in animals capable of limb regeneration. This project investigates the origins of these master regulators in synovial cells, the mechanisms involved, and how aging effects these mechanisms in human joint tissue.

application_id: 11071771
activity_code: F31
title: How genome architecture controls Protocadherin gene choice at single-allele resolution
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Clustered Protocadherin (Pcdh) genes confer molecular identities to neurons that allow them to determine when they are contacting neuronal branches from the same or different cells. The ability of neurons to distinguish self from non-self is essential to their morphological and physiological development, and accordingly, dysregulation of Pcdh genes has been linked to several neuropsychiatric and neurological disorders. The findings from this proposal will generate a molecular understanding of how Pcdh genes are regulated across neurodevelopment, which could enable the development of therapeutics that remedy the defects in neural wiring seen in these disorders.

activity_code: K99
title: Neurocomputational mechanisms of serotonin, sustained stress, and mental effort allocation
org_name: BROWN UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative This project proposal uses computational modeling, psychopharmacology, and pharmaco-fMRI to investigate the neural and computational mechanisms by which people allocate mental effort when faced with sustained and uncontrollable stress. These data will provide the first direct and multi-modal examination of how people process the controllability of sustained stressors to guide dissociable strategies for allocating mental effort. Collectively, this research will provide an important foundation for understanding the computations, networks, and neurotransmitter systems that drive effortful thoughts and actions, and when and why they fail to do so (e.g., motivational impairments in depression, anxiety, OCD, Alzheimer’s).

application_id: 10908818
activity_code: DP2
title: Editing Population Codes For Action
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative:
Complex, time varying, patterns of neural activity are thought to give rise to all of the behavioral complexity that
animals engage in. However, technical limitations have made it impossible to recreate these patterns de novo
in vivo, and thus we poorly understand how these patterns causally drive different movements. In this proposal,
we use a unique and novel technique to drive precise, multi-neuronal, patterns of activity in awake animals to
understand what features of neural activity drive action and ultimately to understand neural population coding.

title: Functions of Rapidly-Evolving Proteins and Their Roles in Pathogenicity
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER

title: The Role of Mitochondria in Inclusion Body Myositis
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite being the most common muscle disease after the age of 50, inclusion body myositis (IBM) remains of
unclear etiology and refractory to any form of treatment, with almost all patients eventually becoming

title: Digital Tools for Precision Nephrology
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposal aims to establish robust phenotyping pipelines for early detection of Collagen Type IV-Associated Nephropathy (COL4A-AN) using electronic health record (EHR) data. application_id: 10880368
activity_code: F30
title: Mechanisms of genomic instability, tumor initiation and progression following the disruption of the RTF2-RNase H2 axis
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative The repair of DNA is vital to maintain the stability of our genetic material, as well as prevent the formation of mutations that can lead to the initiation and progression of malignancy. This proposal seeks to 1) gain insight into how the removal of ribonucleotides from the genome is regulated and 2) propose a novel method allowing the quantitative study of ribonucleotide incorporation in human cells. Completion of this work will shed light into how the enzyme RNase H2, and enzyme exhibiting frequent copy number loss in a Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia, maintains genomic stability and prevents transformation to malignancy.

org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative von Willebrand factor (vWF) is essential for normal hemostasis and thrombosis and is involved in other important processes such as angiogenesis and cancer cell metastasis. title: Functional Analysis of the Role of Transcription Factor 21 (Tcf21) in Nephron Progenitor Cells in Congenital Anomalies of the Kidney and Urinary Tract
org_name: LURIE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Kidney birth defects, which account for 25% of all birth defects in humans, are the leading cause of chronic renal disease in children and young adults. In the absence of curative treatment, patients rely on dialysis and kidney transplantation and consequently have significant shorter lifespan. In this project, we will model kidney birth defects to study the mechanisms that cause abnormal differentiation of renal cells, with the intention that this knowledge will advance the application of therapeutic strategies that depends on stem cell regeneration.

activity_code: K22
title: Neuroimmune Mechanisms of Cognitive Impairment in Salt-sensitive Hypertension
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Hypertension is a leading risk factor for cognitive impairment and dementia, a condition affecting almost 50 million adults worldwide, and a major cause of death and disability. This application seeks to determine the mechanisms underlying the damaging effects of hypertension on the brain by focusing on salt-sensitivity and immune signaling, key features of human hypertension. By identifying the molecules and cells involved in the harmful effects of hypertension on the brain, our studies have the potential to provide novel therapeutic insights to protect the brain from the effects of high blood pressure.

title: Defining the cell type-specific role of miR-9-2 in telencephalon development
org_name: SEATTLE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Dysregulation of gene networks during development can lead to organ malformation, dysfunction and disease, including neurological diseases such as schizophrenia, Huntington’s and Alzheimer’s disease. activity_code: DP2
title: The molecular basis of ferrosome organelle biogenesis and its impact on host-microbe interactions
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE: Our prior research and preliminary data have demonstrated that the Gram-positive anaerobic pathogen Clostridioides difficile produces membrane-bound ferrosome organelles as part of its defense strategy against host-imposed iron sequestration; however, the molecular basis of ferrosome formation remains poorly understood. The experiments outlined in this application aim to elucidate the underlying mechanisms of ferrosome biogenesis, define the function of the ferrosome iron minerals within the gut community, and determine the impact of nutrient iron on host-microbe interactions. The findings of this proposal will uncover novel factors critical for C. difficile infection and create a framework for developing effective antimicrobial therapeutics to combat this important infection.

application_id: 10894644
activity_code: F32
title: Dissecting cholinergic modulation of interneurons underlying state-dependent processing in mouse visual cortex
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Viewing the same scene or object can drive different responses in visual cortex depending on whether the observer is stationary or moving, engaged or passive. Enabling this context-dependent processing is thought to be a key function of the neuromodulator acetylcholine, and deficits of acetylcholine are associated with disorders ranging from Alzheimer's Disease to schizophrenia; however, the mechanisms through which acetylcholine modulates the cortex are incompletely understood, hampering the development of treatments. This proposal dissects the action of acetylcholine on specific cell types in the visual cortex, clarifying how this neuromodulator impacts components of the cortical circuity to drive context-specific processing.

application_id: 10909340
activity_code: F99
title: Dissecting the tumor cell-immune TME axis to identify therapeutically actionable vulnerabilities that potentiate immunotherapy in GBM
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Glioblastoma multiforme, a common and highly aggressive form of brain cancer with a universally fatal outcome, is characterized by a vast degree of heterogeneity and an immune-suppressive tumor microenvironment driven by cancer stem cells. Our preliminary results show that glioma cancer stem cells activate cell-intrinsic mechanisms to drive and maintain a unique, immunosuppressive cell phenotype and suggest that targeting immunosuppressive GBM cells will boost the anti-tumor immune response. Favorable results from this proposal will provide a rationale for combining therapeutics directed at immunosuppressive GBM cells with other immunotherapies (e.g. checkpoint inhibitors, CAR-T cells, vaccines) to treat brain tumors.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Differential RNA-sequencing of temporal cortex autopsy samples from typical and atypical forms of the neurodevelopmental disorder Rett syndrome (RTT) has uncovered elevated heat shock signaling as a highly conserved aspect of the disease. application_id: 10734762
activity_code: F30
title: The role of mitochondrial dysfunction in age-related disease: a human genetic approach
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative Mitochondrial dysfunction has been associated with aging and with virtually all age-related diseases (e.g., neurodegenerative disease, cardiovascular disease), however it remains unclear how this dysfunction arises and if it truly causes these conditions. This objective of this project is to use state-of-the-art human genetics approaches to understand (1) the association landscape linking mitochondrial dysfunction and age-related disease, (2) if this dysfunction is causal for the disease, and (3) the genetic architecture underlying the dysfunction itself. This proposal has the potential to provide clarity on the mechanisms underlying aging, age- related disease and mitochondrial dysfunction, potentially nominating new therapeutic targets and strategies.

title: Mechanisms of Thyroid Autoimmunity in Checkpoint Immunotherapy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Immune checkpoint inhibitors are a power new form of cancer treatment, but their benefits are limited by the development of autoimmune side effects in many patients. Thyroid disease is one of the most common of these autoimmune toxicities and has both overlapping and distinct features from other forms of thyroid autoimmune disease (e.g. Hashimoto’s thyroiditis). activity_code: K76
title: Optimal Preoperative Care for Older Adults with Inflammatory Bowel Disease (K76) - Resubmission - 1
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: NARRATIVE
Surgery is often deferred for older adults with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) given the higher risk of
adverse surgical outcomes as compared to younger adults with IBD. These delays, however, paradoxically
increase the risk of an adverse postoperative event when surgery is required. A better understanding of which
older adults with IBD are likely to need disease-related surgery, which preoperative factors contribute to the
risk of adverse surgical outcomes, and which barriers exist for timely surgery can shift this trajectory and help
inform evidence-based interventions.

title: Regulation of Lymphatic and Vascular Remodeling in Acute Kidney Injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) is a serious clinical problem that affects ~20% of hospitalized patients and 60% of critically ill patients while increasing risk of developing chronic kidney disease. The overall goal of this project is to understand cellular and molecular mechanisms that govern how lymphatic and blood endothelial cells in the kidney respond to injury and impact AKI pathogenesis. application_id: 10985817
activity_code: K99
title: Systemic travel of damaged DNA in photosensitive autoimmune disorders
org_name: WRIGHT STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Environmental exposure to ultraviolet (UV) radiation mediates systemic inflammatory responses in susceptible individuals with photosensitive autoimmune disease, yet mechanisms of signal transduction are poorly understood. We present evidence that exposure to UVB results in release of photoproduct-containing DNA in extracellular vesicles, and photoproducts can be detected in the lymphatic systems of mice and in urine of human subjects. The proposed research seeks to understand the mode of circulation and impact of extracellular damaged DNA in the context of photosensitive autoimmune disease and contribute to better diagnostics and treatments for sensitizing exposures to environmental genotoxins.

application_id: 10985636
activity_code: K23
title: A Family-Based Digital Intervention to Address Early Substance Misuse among Black Adolescents
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  This study will develop and test a novel culturally tailored family-based videogame, FamilyBond, to prevent worsening substance misuse among urban Black adolescents in community-based primary care settings by addressing both universal and unique risk and protective factors for this population. Findings from the study will provide the first effective family-based digital intervention to lower intentions to misuse substances among urban Black adolescents. title: Investigating molecular regulators of a mixed-lineage state in lung adenocarcinoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) is the most common cause of cancer mortality worldwide and is difficult to target therapeutically due to substantial heterogeneity and lineage plasticity that arises during progression and therapeutic response. title: Modulation of the biliary immune niche by the microbiome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Biliary tract inflammation contributes to the development and progression of many common human biliary and liver pathologies, but our understanding of immune processes within the biliary tract is exceedingly limited. I have characterized resident immune cell populations in the extrahepatic biliary tree at high resolution, and found that the make-up of this biliary immune niche is impacted by biliary epithelial cells and the microbiome-status of the host. application_id: 10868594
activity_code: F30
title: Quantitative Magnetization Transfer Imaging for Early Detection of Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative  This project aims to develop a rapid and robust quantitative magnetization transfer imaging method sensitive to early in vivo amyloid beta pathology in cognitively normal individuals. This innovation is enabled by the use of two key technologies: the hybrid spin-ensemble state and unrolled neural networks. Successful completion of this project will establish quantitative magnetization transfer as a viable technique for tracking Alzheimer's disease progression and monitor patient's responses to novel drug therapies.

title: A Framework for the Social Impact of Algorithms in Health Care
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Algorithms continue to proliferate in the health care system, often with dire consequences for marginalized groups. This research proposes building a framework for evaluating the social impact of algorithms in health care before they are deployed to avoid such harms using mathematical decision science modeling. activity_code: F31
title: Molecular mechanism of membrane association of Bruton's Tyrosine Kinase
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE (3 sentences) Pharmaceuticals for treatment of B cell malignancies rely on a class of inhibitors that irreversibly bind the ATP-binding cleft of Bruton's Tyrosine Kinase. Due to their covalent binding within this pocket, these inhibitors have significant off-target side effects and are susceptible to resistance mutations. The goal of this project is to elucidate the lipid-binding and oligomerization of Btk to understand the mechanism of membrane association as potential alternative sites and pathways for Btk inhibition.

application_id: 10764264
activity_code: K01
title: Role of the circadian rhythm on endocannabinoid response to an acute stress
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative Endocannabinoids, lipid messengers, are a principal modulator of stress, regulate emotions and cardiovascular function, and have diurnal rhythmicity. In order to determine how the large daily fluctuation in endocannabinoid signaling at rest impacts emotional and cardiovascular reactivity in response to an abrupt stressor, we will use a circadian protocol that allows us to equally distribute all behaviors such as stress exposure, meals, and sleep, across the circadian cycle. Our hypothesized differential endocannabinoid and associated cardiovascular response to a stress across the circadian system provides a plausible pathway that could underlie risks for development of cardiovascular disease in night and rotating shift workers.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Type 1 diabetes (T1D) occurs when a loss of immune tolerance to insulin-producing beta cells of the pancreas leads to their destruction and blood glucose dysregulation. T1D can be cured with beta cell replacement through pancreatic islet transplantation, but the need for chronic systemic immunosuppression limits the applicability of this procedure. Islet transplantation without the need for chronic systemic immunosuppression can be achieved by encapsulation, where transplanted islets are coated in biocompatible gels that act as a barrier to recipient immune responses. Unfortunately, an indirect immune response still occurs. This proposed project seeks to combine islet encapsulation with two tolerance-inducing therapies that block this indirect immune response, thereby imp...

org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Recently discharged psychiatric inpatients who misuse alcohol are at high risk for suicide. The proposed project involves the development of a novel intervention that would enhance care for these individuals by pairing inpatient-based emotion regulation skills training with a mobile health application that extends this treatment into the post-discharge period. Through acquisition and eventual utilization of emotion regulation skills to replace maladaptive, stress-induced emotion-driven behaviors (e.g., heavy drinking, suicide attempts), this approach could reduce post-discharge risk for a suicide crisis and costly rehospitalization.

title: Uncovering the Molecular Determinants of Cell Type-Specific Connectivity
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: Project Narrative To form the brain circuits that will eventually carry out all neural functions, different types of neurons must precisely wire together. application_id: 10914989
activity_code: K23
title: The Gut-Liver Axis in HIV-Related Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Narrative Persons with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) have higher risk of developing fatty liver disease (NAFLD) than HIV-negative persons but the reasons for this discrepancy are not known. Changes in the intestinal microbiome may contribute to the development of NAFLD in persons with HIV (PWH) through impairment of barrier function of the intestinal wall and by producing metabolites that are harmful to the liver. This project will test the hypothesis that HIV-related NAFLD is associated with differences in the intestinal microbiome and that supplementation with probiotic and prebiotic fiber will lead to improvements in markers of NAFLD in PWH.

activity_code: K23
title: Refining neurophysiological biomarkers of epilepsy using deep learning to guide pediatric epilepsy surgery
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE More than one-third of children with epilepsy do not respond to medications and are therefore candidates for epilepsy surgery. title: Neurocognitive mechanisms of reward - motivated memory persistence across age
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Substance use initiation during adolescence is often associated with risky behaviors driven by heightened reward sensitivity, but increased susceptibility to substance abuse may also depend in part on the persistence of reward-associated memories. This proposal aims to determine how reward states – both in the lab and in the real-world – modulate lasting memories that can carry over to guide future behaviors from childhood to adulthood. title: Low-dose buccal buprenorphine: Relative abuse potential and postoperative analgesic acceptability
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: NARRATIVE Post-surgical opioids contribute substantially to the burden of opioid misuse, opioid use disorder, and overdose, which caused nearly 70,000 U.S. deaths in 2020. title: EGF Receptor and Notch Signaling in the Pathogenesis of Menetrier's Disease
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Ménétrier’s disease is an acquired premalignant hypertrophic gastropathy caused by EGF receptor (EGFR) signaling activation in which the EGFR neutralizing monoclonal antibody, cetuximab, is the first effective medical therapy. My identification of Notch signaling downstream of EGFR signaling in Ménétrier’s disease has led to the design of present studies that will dissect the role of EGFR and Notch signaling in two cardinal features of the disease (loss of parietal cells and foveolar hyperplasia) and optimize pharmacological blockade of EGFR and Notch signaling to improve the treatment of affected individuals. application_id: 10870166
activity_code: K01
title: Health and economic impact analysis of a reduced nicotine cigarette policy on vulnerable populations
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The US Food and Drug Administration is considering reducing nicotine levels in cigarettes so that they are nonaddictive, which could have long-term implications for vulnerable populations that are disproportionately harmed by smoking. This study will use simulation modeling methods to conduct health and economic impact analysis of a reduced nicotine cigarette policy for two vulnerable populations; people with major depression and socioeconomically disadvantaged women of reproductive age. The policy will furthermore be assessed in the context of e-cigarette use and a potential illicit market for normal nicotine cigarettes.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE After injury, zymogenic cells of the gastrointestinal tract (e.g., pancreatic acinar and gastric chief cells) can become metaplastic, and then be recruited back into the cell cycle for regeneration of lost cells through a potentially carcinogenic process known as paligenosis. activity_code: F31
title: Investigating Ensemble Activity Dynamics of a Thalamo-Striatal Circuit Regulating Alcohol Seeking
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alcohol use disorder is classically characterized by a loss of behavioral control, particularly in the face of negative consequences, resulting in a loss of inhibitory control that may normally suppress alcohol seeking. application_id: 10825697
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the mechanism and role of the steady-state CXCR3-CXCL10 gut-microbiota-brain T cell axis
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: The microbiome and the gut-brain axis has been demonstrated to mediate the symptoms and progression of a wide variety of neurological disease1-6. While the role of the gut-microbiota-T-cell-brain axis in the context of specific neurological diseases has been studied, its role at steady-state and scope of neuroimmune interactions between microbiota-educated-T cells and microglia is not known. It is essential to understand the steady-state gut-brain T cell axis in order to fully understand the molecular mechanisms behind neurological disease to develop better targeted therapeutics.

activity_code: K00
title: What Mechanisms Underlie Top-Down Modulation of Human Visual Cortex?
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE What we perceive and hold in working memory is dependent on selective attention — a top-down cognitive ability that regulates the limited cortical resources available in the human brain from moment to moment. The proposed work will use a combination of cutting-edge non-invasive human brain imaging (fMRI and EEG), combined with computational modeling, to reveal the neural computational mechanisms through which attention regulates limited visuocortical resources. Understanding the neural substrates underlying attention in healthy adults is paramount for developing targeted interventions for brain disorders that disrupt attention, perception, and working memory — crucial cognitive functions in our everyday lives.

activity_code: F31
title: Role of 14-3-3θ in Environmental Toxicant Exposure and Neuroinflammation in Synucleinopathies
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE 14-3-3θ phosphorylation at S232 is markedly elevated within insoluble fractions of human brains with synucleinopathy, including Parkinson’s Disease (PD) and Dementia with Lewy Bodies (DLB). title: The role of mucosal serotonin in visceral nociception and gut motility
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a prevalent disorder characterized by visceral (e.g., abdominal) pain and disordered motility (e.g., constipation and/or diarrhea) for which there are limited effective treatments. application_id: 10900085
activity_code: F31
title: The role of P/Q-type channels in oligodendrocyte lineage cell development
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative In this study, I will determine the cell-autonomous and non-autonomous roles of the P/Q-type channel, a voltage- gated calcium channel associated with CACNA1A-related neurological disorders including ataxia, epilepsy, and intellectual disability, in oligodendrocyte lineage cells (OLCs), the myelinating glia of the central nervous system. To do this, I will use cell-specific and global CRISPR/Cas9 mutagenesis, in vivo imaging of transgenic zebrafish, motor assays, and a newly developed protocol to perform whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology in zebrafish spinal cord OLCs. Together, these experiments will provide new insight into the signaling properties of developing OLCs and the role of OLCs in CACNA1A-related disorders, which may uncover new therapeutic targets.

application_id: 10903622
activity_code: F31
title: Unraveling alphavirus neuroinvasion: Molecular insights from a stem cell based blood-brain barrier model
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alphaviruses like Sindbis (SINV) and chikungunya (CHIKV) virus, can cause encephalitis by crossing the blood- brain barrier, likely via infection of the brain microvascular endothelial cells that make up the barrier. Seminal work in the field has identified residues in the SINV and CHIKV E2 glycoproteins that are critical for neuroinvasion in vivo, while additional work has separately identified host receptors that interact with SINV and CHIKV E2. We expand upon these findings by characterizing the relationship between these E2 residues and known E2 interactors at the blood-brain barrier, thus laying the groundwork for novel treatments against the increasing threat of a large scale CHIKV outbreak.

title: The Role of G Protein-Coupled Estrogen Receptor Signaling in the Maintenance of Beta Cell Identity
org_name: PURDUE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) is a global health pandemic that results from impaired insulin secretion, peripheral insulin resistance, obesity, overnutrition, lack of physical activity, and aging. However, genetic and physiologic data suggest that inadequate insulin secretion from the pancreatic beta () cell is the chief determinant predicting progression from insulin resistance to overt T2D. The goal of this research is to understand the mechanistic interplay between the process of store operated calcium entry (SOCE) and estrogen signaling in the  cell and will test the hypothesis that decreased SOCE leads to reduced G Protein-Coupled Estrogen Receptor signaling and loss of β cell identity and function, thereby contributing to the pathophysiology of T2D.

title: The Role of CSF Dynamics in Infant Brain and Behavioral Development in Down Syndrome and Related Neurodevelopmental Disorders

org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: Project Narrative
Nucleic acids, such as DNA and RNA, exist in cells as highly complex molecular structures subject to numerous
modifications and vulnerable to damage, either of which can change their function and impact cellular states;
however current sequencing technology can only analyze their base sequence, ignoring much of this information.
Here, I present preliminary data and propose a path to expand a technique developed by my lab to directly
sequence modified or damaged DNA or RNA, using an innovative approach to modify novel sequencing
technology. With these tools, we hope to understand the processes that converge on DNA and RNA, such as
chemotherapy in cancer or natural damage that occurs during aging or neurodegeneration, to better learn how
cells modify, repair, and interpret ...

activity_code: K24
title: Clinical Impacts of Heterogeneous Interferon Responses to Viral Infection by Asthmatic Airway Epithelium
org_name: SEATTLE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Using airway epithelial cells from clinically well characterized asthmatic children, we will investigate the regulation of epithelial interferon responses to viral infection and the impacts of excessive or deficient epithelial interferon responses on incident viral triggered asthma exacerbation risk and lung function over time. The studies conducted in my research program, and unique resource of primary airway epithelial cells from clinically well characterized asthmatic children, provide an exceptional training environment for young investigators dedicated to a career in mechanistic patient-oriented research to learn translational research techniques to investigate the role of the airway epithelium in pediatric lung diseases and viral infection.

application_id: 10911251
activity_code: K08
title: Tracing the Evolution of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia to Richter's Syndrome: Defining Transformation
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative Richter’s syndrome (RS) is an aggressive malignancy of B cells that arises in a subset of patients with the otherwise typically indolent chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), and dramatically limits survival in CLL patients. Despite advances in tumor sequencing techniques and analytic tools, the genetic basis of RS remains largely uncharacterized. The objective of the proposed studies is to identify and trace the origins of RS arising from CLL and define its molecular features in order to gain a deeper understanding of the subtypes of RS—which is anticipated to improve both the diagnosis and therapy of RS.

application_id: 10912476
activity_code: F32
title: Peptide-Conjugated Palladium Oxidative Addition Complexes for Site-Selective Arylation Chemistry
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The site-selective functionalization of biomolecules including proteins and complex biologically active small molecules represents a frontier in modern bioconjugation. The following proposal details the use of peptide- conjugated palladium oxidative addition complexes to modify important protein and biologically active small molecule targets in a highly modular and general approach. Leveraging the ability of user-defined peptide sequences to direct the C–heteroatom bond-forming chemistry of palladium complexes will allow for site- selective “traceless” modification of biomolecules, which is expected to enable a powerful discovery platform for chemical biology and the preparation of novel therapeutics.

application_id: 10913306
activity_code: F31
title: The role of tumor cell-of-origin-specific PDL1 on tumorigenesis and tumor progression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCIENCE CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Here we study tumor-intrinsic PDL1 signals in early carcinogenesis and progression using a novel autochthonous murine model in which mice develop NrasQ61R-mutant melanomas accelerated by UV exposure that lack PDL1 in the tumor cell-of-origin from initiation, definitively distinguishing biologic PDL1 signals versus compensatory mechanisms that could confound prior genetic PDL1KO studies. Our work defining tumor-intrinsic PDL1 signals in early carcinogenesis and their contribution to tumor progression and the tumor microenvironment provides insights into novel targetable ways in which PDL1 suppresses antitumor immunity. Our data can help improve melanoma treatments or response biomarkers.

title: Acoustic Modeling of skull bone for improved transcranial MR-guided focused ultrasound therapy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative: Magnetic resonance-guided focused ultrasound (MRgFUS) is a promising non-invasive treatment modality being investigated for various disorders. application_id: 10908636
activity_code: DP2
title: High-spatial-resolution multi-omics sequencing of brain lesions in multiple sclerosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative This project aims to develop a scalable and transformative technology for simultaneous profiling of multi epigenomic modalities, whole transcriptome, and a panel of proteins at tissue scale and cellular level in a spatially resolved manner. It will be applied to brain lesions in multiple sclerosis to generate detailed spatial map of relevant cells, including interactions among them, and to identify cellular and molecular mechanisms operating in MS brain lesions. It will improve our understanding of the relationship between tissue organization, function, and gene regulatory networks in MS.

title: The effects of noradrenergic activity on age-related distractibility
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative Age-related cognitive impairments co-occur with the disruption of molecular signaling within the brain, but the basic mechanisms of this relationship are unclear. activity_code: K23
title: Targeting Emotion Dysregulation to Reduce Suicide in People with Psychosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Project Narrative
People with psychotic disorders are excluded from most suicide-focused clinical trials despite incredibly high rates of
completed suicide, and interventions that have been tested for this population have shown limited impact on suicide.
Emotion dysregulation is a significant suicidogenic factor that is understudied in people with psychotic disorders. We
propose to implement and evaluate an intervention targeting emotion dysregulation in people with psychotic disorders
while employing rigorous statistical modeling and measurement techniques including Ecological Momentary
Assessment, which will support future advanced research on suicide and suicide prevention for this high-risk group.

phr: The proposed project compares the stress, anthropometric, biological, and epigenetic age profiles of African American and African ancestry older adults across age cohorts (young-old and long-lived) in both the Health and Retirement Study (HRS) and the Study of Longevity and Stress in African American Families (SOLSAA). This project will improve measures of aging and support a paradigm shift in aging research, combining medical frameworks with social stress frameworks to improve our understanding of the aging process.

application_id: 10928750
activity_code: F30
title: Investigating the impact of chronic stress on distinct axes of dopamine signaling
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative. Adaptive functioning in all animals, including humans, requires a careful balance between recognition and pursuit of potential rewards (food, shelter, and social interaction, among others) and detection and avoidance of potential threats (theft, natural disasters, and interpersonal rejection, for instance). Many psychiatric diseases involve impairments in one or both of these processes, symptoms which significantly impair functioning and cause considerable distress. The present work will investigate the neural basis of alterations in reward and threat processing following chronic stress in mice, with the goal of understanding and ameliorating the changes in brain circuitry that lead to impairments in reward and threat processing.

title: Harnessing cell-to-cell variability to understand viral infection outcomes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE While it is now accepted that cell-to-cell variability affects viral infection outcome, we currently lack mechanistic understanding as to the host factors and transcriptional programs that control it. phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Hazardous alcohol use is highly prevalent among the millions of older adults who have chronic pain and contributes to greater pain severity, physical impairments, and the exacerbation of chronic pain over time. title: High-dimensional chemical targeting of multiple neuron types to regulate energy balance
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative This high-risk, high-reward proposal will test whether we can use novel machine-learning methods to design drug mixtures that jointly activate or inhibit, respectively, the activity of neurons that increase or decrease satiety, while sparing off-target neurons types to reduce side effects. org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Over 38 million people in the world live with HIV and are at greater risk of developing cardiovascular diseases, even when receiving treatment for HIV. Because people living with HIV receive medications that affect the body’s metabolism towards developing cardiovascular diseases, we will use state-of-the-art methodologies to determine the relationship between exposure from these drugs and important metabolism markers. We will identify the effects of drug exposure and pioneer a new area of HIV/AIDS research and more reliably predict the development of cardiovascular diseases for those living with HIV.

title: A Virtual, Group-Based, Expressive Writing Intervention for Survivors of Adolescent and Young Adult Cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Adolescent and young adult cancer survivors (AYACSs) encounter psychosocial challenges that are often not addressed by existing interventions, leading to disparities in their quality of life (QOL). In response, the proposed study aims to develop and evaluate a virtual, group-based expressive writing intervention that takes into account developmental stages and communication preferences of AYACSs to improve their QOL and reduce stress. If successful, this intervention will be ready for widespread expansion, including to underserved communities with limited resources and support.

title: Development of a Predictive Response Signature to Anti-Thymocyte Globulin in Type 1 Diabetes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG), an immunotherapy, preserves β-cell function in type 1 diabetes (T1D). However,
not all treated individuals boast a similar response, necessitating immune characterization of these clinical
“responders”. Identification of a response signature could be applied to future clinical trial enrollment criteria—to
personalize care and direct patients to an immune therapy with the highest likelihood of success (β-cell
preservation).

application_id: 10949957
activity_code: K23
title: Optimizing Implementation of Digital X-ray with Computer-Aided Detection for Community-Based Tuberculosis Screening
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Computer-aided detection (CAD), which uses artificial intelligence to analyze chest X-rays, is now WHO- recommended for tuberculosis (TB) screening and holds the potential to improve TB detection in high-burden, low-resource communities. This proposed research seeks to evaluate the adaptive application of CAD for community-based TB screening in sub-Saharan Africa, utilizing individualized and context-specific methods, as an alternative to the current standard of a "one-size-fits-all" approach. By assessing diagnostic accuracy, implementation barriers, and cost-effectiveness of each screening strategy, the insights gained from this study could be used to facilitate earlier detection of TB and reduce the high morbidity and mortality caused by tuberculosis.

activity_code: K00
title: Plasticity of cortical circuits in health, aging, and Alzheimer's disease
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative  The cerebral cortex is instrumental to all human and animal cognition, ranging from sensation to decision making to movement. The complexity of cortical circuits is a major obstacle to both understanding cortical function as a whole and to treating maladies that might afflict it, including numerous neurological and psychiatric disorders. The proposed studies will reveal how cortical circuits interact to execute cortical computations, how these interactions are impaired by aging and neurodegeneration, and how we can noninvasively stimulate cortical circuits to galvanize the brain to fight against pathologies caused by aging and Alzheimer’s disease.

title: Automated Arthroscopic Partial Meniscectomy Patient Outcome Prediction using Deep Learning
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: Project Narrative Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy provides no clinically meaningful benefits for certain patient groups even after physical therapy fails. application_id: 11215269
activity_code: K76
title: Genetic architecture of age of onset of amyloid positivity: Integrating imaging and genetic tools to identify novel drivers of disease
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ARIZONA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE. Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the sixth leading cause of death in the United States, affecting almost 6 million people which is expected to more than double by mid-century. The neuropathologic hallmarks of AD including β-amyloid (Aβ) plaques and tau neurofibrillary tangles accumulate in the brain decades before symptom onset, and drive downstream neurodegeneration, cognitive impairment, and death. Identifying genetic drivers of early neuropathologic changes like conversion to Aβ positivity (Aβ+) may lead to novel therapeutic targets useful earlier in the disease cascade when treatment is most effective.

application_id: 10878553
activity_code: F99
title: Investigating the susceptibility of basal forebrain cholinergic neurons in individuals with Down syndrome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Basal forebrain cholinergic neurons (BFCNs) are important for arousal, attention, learning, and memory, and they are prone to degeneration in Down syndrome (DS) and other neurodegenerative diseases. The F99 proposal will determine if there are fewer BFCNs at birth in DS and utilize iPSC models while the K00 proposal will expand this work to determine the temporal order of pathology accumulation in the DS basal forebrain and identify molecular mechanisms that may contribute to BFCN susceptibility. A better understanding of the role of BFCNs in disease progression may improve DS and other neurodegenerative disease models as well as elucidate novel therapeutic targets.

title: Dissecting distinct contributions of different Prefrontal subregions on goal-directed visual attention
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: The prefrontal cortex is the part of the brain that hold our cognitive abilities, and it is thought to communicate
with our visual system to produce attention towards visual information important for our current needs. In this
proposal, we will study the nature of this communication to understand how we choose to attend to discrete
aspects of our surroundings. With a thorough understanding of the underlying neuronal mechanisms, we can
begin to understand how this communication malfunctions, and attention falls short, in disorders like attention
deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and Schizophrenia.

activity_code: F30
title: Clinically feasible functional MRI providing independent assessments of cerebrovascular stiffness and microcirculation in typical aging and Alzheimer's Disease cohorts
org_name: PURDUE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE It is known that cerebrovascular health is a significant risk factor for Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) dementia, the current leading cause of dementia worldwide. In this project, we will develop cerebrovascular health metrics from resting-state functional MRI data of the human connectome project to first understand cerebrovascular changes in a typical aging population and then apply these metrics to a large AD dataset. This project aims to develop reproducible measures of cerebrovascular health and then assess the association of these metrics with the cognitive impairments seen in AD dementia.
