application_id: 10906192
activity_code: K25
title: Machine learning-based methods for phenotyping dementia patients from electronic health record data
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Electronic health record (EHR) data holds immense potential for the study and care of Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRDs) through the development of clinical tools and the study of disease heterogeneity and current care practices. However, selection bias, measurement error, and missing data can all lead to bias in EHR-based studies and tools, especially among patients who face barriers to healthcare access. The proposed training and research plan would lead to EHR analysis methods for ADRDs that address these sources of bias and would provide Dr. Adams with the unique combination of advance computational skills and understanding of ADRD care necessary to make significant contributions to the ADRD precision medicine in the future.

application_id: 11141927
activity_code: K23
title: Toward optimizing dialysis in the perioperative period
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Persons with kidney disease are 16 times more likely to need surgery and have a higher risk of complications
from surgery compared to patients with normal or near normal kidney function. This proposal will study the link
between how we perform hemodialysis before surgery and complications after surgery, including heart attack,
stroke, heart failure, irregular heart rhythms, or infections. Completion of this work will provide health care
providers with important information about how to prevent complications when persons living with kidney
disease need surgery.

title: Developing a sleep health intervention for shift workers
org_name: OREGON STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Poor sleep health is an underappreciated risk factor for disease, death, errors, and accidents among shift workers, who make up 20% of the US workforce. The purpose of the proposed K23 is to prepare the applicant for an independent clinical sleep research career by developing, refining, and pilot-testing a comprehensive, personalized, media-augmented telehealth intervention designed to improve sleep health among shift workers. This proposal will address the critical lack of evidence-based, accessible interventions by targeting shift workers' unique sleep health problems which manifest during both sleep (e.g., insomnia symptoms, short sleep duration) and wake (e.g., fatigue, sleepiness, poor executive function, depressive symptoms).

title: Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy for the chronic pain-depression co-morbidity among older Blacks in the community; The Quiet Focus study
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Co-morbid chronic pain-depression is common among older Blacks and this co-morbidity worsens physical and emotional function. Access to evidence based non-pharmacological management is limited by systemic racism, stigma, and the lack of culturally-tailored care. Mindfulness based cognitive therapy (MBCT) is an evidence-based, non-pharmacological intervention that could address the social-determinants of the chronic pain-depression co-morbidity among older Blacks, but it requires tailoring. The proposed study will establish the feasibility, acceptability and credibility of Quiet Focus, a cultural adaptation of MBCT aimed at the chronic pain-depression co-morbidity among older Blacks in the community.

application_id: 11129620
activity_code: F30
title: Role of cytoskeletal interactions with mechanogated ion channel Piezo2 in the mechanism of mechanotransduction by enteroendocrine cells
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative
Deficiencies in mechanosensation contribute to functional gastrointestinal diseases (FGIDs) like irritable bowel
syndrome (IBS), which collectively affect around 15% of US adults, but mechanosensation has not been
directly targeted for treatment because of poor mechanistic understanding of how physical forces influence GI
function. Our lab discovered mechanosensitive enteroendocrine cells (EECs) in the GI epithelium that release
physiologically active hormones in response to force, so in this proposal we seek to uncover the mechanism by
which mechanosensitive proteins work together to make EECs efficient force sensors. Acquiring this
knowledge may advance our understanding of mechanosensation in sensory epithelia, and pinpoint targets for
the treatment of FGIDs.

application_id: 11159698
activity_code: K01
title: Circadian regulation of molecular and systemic adipose tissue function in humans
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative
Intrinsic molecular circadian rhythms in adipose tissue control metabolic processes that are dysregulated in
people with specific types of obesity, however, these same functions are highly responsive to intermittent food
intake over the day. As such, these studies will determine the relative contribution of adipose tissue intrinsic
circadian rhythms and intermittent meal delivery on molecular and systemic adipose tissue function in humans
in the context of obesity. By better understanding the relationship between these processes we can develop
new treatments for people with obesity and those with regular disruptions in circadian rhythms such as shift
workers, who have increased risk of obesity and Type 2 diabetes.

activity_code: K99
title: Impact of HIV-associated intestinal CD8+ T cell function in mucosal homeostasis
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Disruption of intestinal homeostasis is a major cause of sustained inflammation in people with HIV (PWH) despite antiretroviral therapy and this leads to increased morbidity and mortality due to inflammatory non-communicable diseases (NCDs) such as diabetes, cardiovascular disease, obesity, and end organ damage. Maintenance of intestinal homeostasis requires coordinated functions of tissue resident immune cells and several epithelial cell lineages; therefore, this proposal will characterize the mechanistic role of intestinal CD8+ T cell immunometabolic function and its contribution to maintaining epithelial homeostasis. Results from this work will provide new insights to develop novel strategies to target mucosal CD8+ T cells and improve health outcomes for PWH.

title: Breaching the Tumor-Immune Mechanical Barrier for Optimal Antitumor Immunity
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Despite the successful development of multiple immunotherapeutic modalities, tumor immune exclusion mechanisms prevent many patients from reaping their benefits. Recent models suggest that tumors actively enforce a mechanical barrier at the tumor-stroma periphery to exclude antitumor immune infiltration and to prevent subsequent immune-mediated killing. This proposal seeks to systematically dissect the components of the tumor-immune mechanical barrier through approaches including in vivo preclinical mouse models, genetic screening, and multi-omics spatial analysis of patient specimens, which will reveal unanticipated immune suppressive mechanisms utilized by cancer and thereby shed new light on novel anticancer treatment strategies.

title: Determining the Injury-Associated Microbial Nutrients that Facilitate Secondary Bacterial Infection in Acutely Injured Lungs
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Independent of initial cause of injury, patients with acutely injured lungs are at increased risk of secondary bacterial lung infection, which prolongs recovery and increases mortality in these patients. We have developed a new approach for culturing bacteria in conditions which mimic the healthy and/or injured lung environment, providing an accurate and convenient way to investigate why the injured lung is more susceptible to secondary bacterial pneumonia. This project aims to determine which nutrients in injured lungs support the growth of pneumonia-associated bacteria, which will provide a scientific basis for the development of clinical interventions that prevent and treat lung infections and, ultimately, improve clinical outcomes for patients with acute lung injury.

title: Novel mechanisms of microRNA-mediated anabolic effects in age-related osteoarthritis
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative We discovered a robust anabolic response in human cartilage that follows a distal-proximal axis gradient (high in the ankle joint and low in the hip joint); this response was modulated by the same master regulators that regulate limb regeneration in animals capable of limb regeneration. activity_code: F31
title: Improving Mental Health Outcomes for Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder: Tailoring Dialectical Behavior Therapy for Adolescents in a Community Mental Health Clinic
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Fetal alcohol spectrum disorders (FASD) affect 2-5% of the school-aged population, with up to 90% of individuals
meeting criteria for a co-occurring mental health disorder. Yet, not a single mental health intervention has been developed
for this population. The current project will adapt Dialectical Behavior Therapy for Adolescents (DBT-A) for individuals
with FASD and conduct a feasibility trial of these adaptations in a community mental health clinic utilizing an
implementation science framework.

title: Deep learning to enable the genetic analysis of aorta
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative Aortic disease is an important contributor to cardiovascular morbidity and sudden death. Key discoveries, including identification of the causal gene for Marfan’s syndrome (FBN1), have advanced our knowledge of syndromic aneurysm and dissection, but to date there remains little information about the genetic basis for sporadic thoracic aortic disease. This proposal aims to expand our understanding of the genetic basis for variation in the properties of the aorta, and to use those factors to predict who may be at high risk of developing clinical aortic disease.

title: Chi gung: a community-based strategy in Chinese immigrant women to improve colorectal and breast cancer screening.
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE While screening has been shown to reduce mortality from colorectal (CRC) and breast cancer (BC), screening uptake remains low among Chinese immigrant women, a group in whom CRC and BC are leading causes of preventable death. application_id: 11136880
activity_code: DP2
title: Decoding the Interferome by Mapping Genetic Interactions in Human Tissue
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Supplemental to its classic role as macrophage activating protein, the cytokine IFN-γ has emerged as a critical
mediator of barrier immunity, mobilizing elaborate transcriptional programs within non-lymphoid tissues to
combat infection. However, the functions, molecular activities, and regulatory circuits of the interferon-stimulated
genes (ISGs) that serve as the effectors of this response are largely a mystery. Focusing on the human lung
epithelium, I develop an experimental pipeline to map a functional genetic interaction network that underpins the
ability of IFN-γ to restrict different classes of pathogens while balancing pathological tissue damage.

activity_code: K23
title: Leveraging mHealth to develop syndemic-based behavioral medicine HIV prevention for the emergency department to reach minoritized PWUD
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite nationally declining rates and efficacious interventions for HIV prevention, services are not reaching
minoritized people who use drugs who continue to face high incidence. Leveraging syndemic theory,
consultation-liaison psychology, emergency departments, and mHealth, the goal of this project is to develop a
low threshold, real-world, multilevel HIV prevention approach. This study is consistent with national Ending the
HIV Epidemic goals and NIH priorities for HIV/AIDS research, by aiming to optimize and improve how we serve
those at high risk for being missed by and/or falling off the HIV prevention cascade.

activity_code: K01
title: Racialization and Cardiovascular Risk Factors among Latinos - An Intersectional Approach
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Some Latino subgroups are at greater risk of heart disease than others based on years in the US, generations in US, and country of origin. Prior large-scale studies assessing these risks have treated this racially diverse population as monolith, failing to capture the variety racial characteristics that can affect how Latino subgroups develop heart disease. We propose to improve how we measure and identify racial subgroups within Latino populations to better understand differences in heart disease risk, permitting specific, targeted solutions for racial health inequities.

title: Developing a nonpharmacological pain intervention for community dwelling older adults with dementia

phr: Project Narrative
Chronic musculoskeletal pain, specifically osteoarthritis (OA) of the knee, is common among community-dwelling people
living with dementia and associated with cascading negative consequences on physical function limitations, quality of life
decline, and caregiver burden; however, few beneficial and appropriate treatment options exist. This study seeks to use
evidence, theory, and stakeholder input to design an exercise-based intervention for knee OA pain management that is
tailored to the interests, preserved abilities, and biopsychosocial-environmental needs of community-dwelling older adults
with mild to moderate dementia. Findings from K23 research will yield a nonpharmacological intervention prototype and
delivery parameters that will inform a future efficacy study an...

application_id: 10800771
activity_code: K01
title: Knee evaluation under mechanical loading by cones ultrashort echo time MR imaging
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The goal of this project is to investigate if the early stage osteoarthritis (OA) affects the mechanical properties of knee joint tissues in an MRI detectable manner prior to morphological alterations. We will perform knee joint evaluation under mechanical loading that mimics our standing position, using novel quantitative MRI techniques that are insensitive to tissue orientation in the MRI scanner. After accelerating our techniques (UTE-MRI), we will determine if the variation pattern of UTE-MRI under loading is distinct for early stage OA patients, and if bone structure for early stage and mild OA knees improves to support the defected knee regions in a detectable manner by MRI and CT.

activity_code: F32
title: The Role of Host Genetics in Shaping Intestinal Lactobacillus Population
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  A host’s physiology is contingent on the composition of their microbiome. While a major player in shaping the commensal population is the host’s genetics, due to insufficient models, the mechanisms by which this occurs is unclear. Herein, we describe our murine model in which host-commensal relationships driven by genetics, independent of environmental factors, can be discovered and the mechanism determined by a multifaceted approach spanning immunology, microbiology, genetics, and computational analyses.

application_id: 10897070
activity_code: K08
title: Leveraging Health Systems to Increase Implementation of Evidence-based Surgical Cancer Care
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Most patients with common cancers in the United States receive surgical care within health systems, but quality is inconsistent across system hub and spoke sites. This project will evaluate the effect of treatment in a health system hub or spoke on receipt of evidence-based surgical cancer care and will identify health system characteristics and strategies that are associated with system performance and consistency across sites. The product, an intervention map for system-level implementation of oncologic evidence, can be used by health systems to provide consistent, high-quality care across sites, increasing equity in cancer care delivery.

title: Focused ultrasound control of intratumoral NKG2DL BTE production by CART cells to potentiate epitope spread
org_name: GEORGIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative To enhance CAR T cells for solid tumors, potent immunomodulators such as cytokines or bispecific T cell engagers (BTEs) can be co-infused. org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Narrative Summary
AIDS-related opportunistic neuro-infections of the brain are a common cause of death and
disability in Sub-Saharan Africa as well as globally. This multidisciplinary training program
focuses on research skills training for post-graduate physician-scientists from universities in
Kampala and Mbarara in Uganda in collaboration with University of Minnesota faculty. This
training program builds on a 17-year history of successful Ugandan and Minnesotan research
partnerships which have impacted international HIV treatment guidelines related to
diagnostics, treatment, and prevention of neuro-infectious diseases.

application_id: 10758542
activity_code: K01
title: Silent Zones of Lung Disease in COPD
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Project Narrative: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is diagnosed by spirometry but significant disagreement exists between spirometric diagnosis and presence of emphysema on computed tomography (CT) due to possibility that a substantial portion of the lung has abnormal mechanics. Using image registration to match inspiratory and expiratory scans point-to-point, we will identify Silent Zones of the lung that appear normal using density thresholds but have abnormal mechanics. The proposed project will use a combination of quantitative imaging and deep learning tools to establish a novel CT biomarker that will enhance diagnosis of clinically significant COPD and also identify individuals at high risk for disease progression.

application_id: 10758558
activity_code: K08
title: Defining the forebrain neurophysiological representation of pain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The severe toll that chronic pain takes on society, made especially clear by the recent opioid epidemic, underscores the need for innovative treatments, particularly for those people with intractable pain who have failed conventional medical and surgical management. Intracranial electrodes implanted for the purpose of epilepsy monitoring provide a rare opportunity to define neurophysiological signatures of pain representations in the human brain and modulate neural circuit activity to attenuate the pain state. Information gained from targeted, high-resolution access to human pain networks can be directly translated to improve existing therapies, including the implantation of deep-brain stimulating electrodes for the treatment of chronic pain.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Healthcare-associated infections in neonatal intensive care units lead to significant morbidity and mortality, and are often caused by antimicrobial resistant pathogens. This proposal will use metagenomic next-generation sequencing in novel ways to track pathogen transmission, identify environmental reservoirs of infection, and understand acquisition of antimicrobial resistant microbes in hospitalized neonates. Together this work leverages innovative metagenomic sequencing tools and analyses to advance infection control approaches and curb the spread of drug-resistant infections.

activity_code: K24
title: Mentoring and Research to ClassifyAtrial Cardiomyopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: The abnormal atrial substrate (atrial cardiomyopathy) is associated with elevated risks of ischemic stroke
dementia, and other cardiovascular outcomes, such as heart failure. Progress in preventing these adverse
outcomes has been hampered by unresolved heterogeneity underlying atrial cardiomyopathy; hence, this K24
grant will support a project that will use machine learning and dense phenotypic data to identify clinically
relevant subtypes of atrial cardiomyopathy. Additionally, this K24 grant will foster a supportive environment for
the mentoring of junior investigators involved in patient-oriented research and facilitate acquisition of new
cutting-edge skills by the PI and his mentees.

title: A Novel Role for Nav1.1 in Mammalian Thermosensation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Project Narrative. How mammalian peripheral sensory neurons encode cold remains poorly defined, despite the essential nature of cold detection to human survival and the prevalence of cold hypersensitivity in several pain etiologies. The goal of this proposal is to systematically and unequivocally define a new role for the voltage- gated sodium channel, NaV1.1, in physiological cold sensing and cold allodynia using approaches that span ion channel biophysics and mouse behavior. Results from this study will deepen our basic understanding of thermal- sensing mechanisms and how they are highjacked during pain.

org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: This application proposes to study the role of nonsense mediated decay in nonsense mutation biology and the pharmacologic correction of nonsense mutations. This proposal uses the human genetic disorder Cystic Fibrosis as a model; however, approximately 10% of all genetic diseases are caused by nonsense mutations which historically have been difficult to treat. title: Neurophysiological Basis for Functional Connectivity Changes in Early Alzheimer’s Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Narrative Individuals at risk of developing Alzheimer’s disease that are amyloid-β positive, but are still cognitively normal, exhibit a characteristic change in brain connectivity prior to the onset of clinical symptoms. title: Acetazolamide to Improve Clinically Important Outcomes in Obstructive Sleep Apnea Patients with a High Loop Gain Endotype
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE

not well tolerated, thus many patients remain untreated. The PI and his mentor/advisor-team believe that

application_id: 10898729
activity_code: K12
title: IRACDA at the University of Pennsylvania: Penn Postdoctoral Opportunities in Research and Teaching (PennPORT).
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Narrative Although the importance of a diverse biomedical science workforce is widely appreciated, individuals from under-represented minority groups continue to comprise a disproportionately small percentage of biomedical researchers in this country. Inspiring undergraduate science teachers can reverse this pattern, but too few scientists are trained as teachers, and even fewer are attuned to the needs of diverse student populations. By combining mentored postdoctoral research at The University of Pennsylvania with training in pedagogy and mentored teaching experiences at partnering minority-serving institutions, the PennPORT IRACDA program trains outstanding researchers and teachers and enhances educational opportunities for students at partner institutions.

title: Understanding the role of dysregulation in positive affect in developmental psychopathology.
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Emotion dysregulation contributes to many psychiatric disorders and results in significant impairment across multiple domains of function from social to health-related outcomes. application_id: 11073024
activity_code: K24
title: Midcareer Investigator Award in Patient-Oriented Research in the Microbiome and Lung Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
When people get infected with viral infections like SARS-CoV-2 and influenza, some get very sick while some
barely notice their symptoms. The goal of this project is to support research that will help us understand if this
variation in patients’ susceptibility to severe disease is explained by differences in the microbiome (the
microbial communities living on and in our bodies). The project will also support the development of a training
program at the University of Michigan for junior investigators interested in conducting patient-oriented research
related to the microbiome, critical illness, and lung disease.

application_id: 10898874
activity_code: K01
title: Control of opioid-motivated approach and avoidance behavior by neuronal ensembles in the basolateral amygdala and their projection targets in the nucleus accumbens
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Opioid Use Disorder (OUD) is a chronic, relapsing disease characterized by compulsive drug seeking and use. This proposal addresses the need for better understanding of the neurocircuitry mediating opioid-motivated approach and avoidance behavior with a focus on groups of projection neurons that are differentially and selectively sensitive to activation by environmental contexts conditioned to morphine (a rewarding opioid) versus naloxone (an aversive opioid antagonist). From a translational perspective, this knowledge may aid in the development of novel treatment strategies acting selectively at signaling or molecular targets unique to opioid- sensitive functional neurons in the brain.

application_id: 10947473
activity_code: K01
title: Diagnosis, Treatment, and Monitoring of Polymicrobial Infections in Diabetic Wounds
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative. Our ongoing study explores the use of Glutamate 1-Semialdehyde (GSA) in inducing porphyrin accumulation in bacteria, facilitating their visual identification and elimination via fluorescence imaging and light-based killing, notably without affecting mammalian cells. While preliminary data showcase the in vitro efficacy of GSA- phototherapy on common wound-infecting bacteria, the proposed research will delve into its detection thresholds, biosafety, and killing efficacy utilizing diabetic mice and mini-pigs. This approach proposes a multifaceted solution for the timely diagnosis, real-time monitoring, and non-antibiotic treatment of bacterial infections in diabetic wounds, potentially preventing severe outcomes like amputation and sepsis.

application_id: 11066510
activity_code: F30
title: Extracellular Matrix Vesicles and Intercellular Communication of Osteoblast-lineage Cells
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Matrix vesicles, small extracellular vesicles released by osteoblasts and anchored in the extracellular matrix, are
enriched with microRNA and other cell signaling molecules that may mediate bone development and
regeneration. The role and mechanism of action of these matrix vesicles in mature bone remains to be
elucidated, but may translate into therapeutic potential for orthopedic pathologies, including critical size bone
defects and chronic nonunions. This study investigates the physical and chemical characteristics of matrix
vesicles and their function with regard to cellular signaling pathway activation and new bone formation, with the
ultimate goal of a novel therapeutic application to improve bone healing.

application_id: 10948161
activity_code: K99
title: Validation and Kinetic Characterization of Blood-Cerebrospinal Fluid Barrier-Traversing Proteins in Aging With Library-Free Multiplexed Targeted Proteomics
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  It is known that blood-brain barrier function declines with aging, but the effects of aging on the choroid plexus, a key component of the blood-brain barrier that transports molecules between the blood and cerebrospinal fluid, are largely unknown. Measuring the extent to which blood-to-brain protein transport in the choroid plexus is altered in aging would reveal which transporters are best targeted as drivers of brain aging or as drug delivery vehicles. This proposal describes using novel mass spectrometry-based proteomic technologies to measure how the kinetics of translocation of transporters and cargo in the choroid plexus change with aging.

application_id: 10949909
activity_code: K99
title: Factors Shaping the Genomic Spectrum of Structural Variants
org_name: BROAD INSTITUTE, INC.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Double strand breaks (DSBs) are a type of DNA damage that is relevant to congenital diseases such as Ataxia telangiectasia, Li Fraumeni syndrome, and Bloom syndrome, as well as cancer and infertility. Incorrect repair of DSBs result in a type of mutation called structural variants (SVs). I will study the impact of mutations in DSB repair genes on the types of SVs in human genomes, which will result in better understanding of mechanisms of DSB repair, and how defects in this repair shape the genome and affect human disease.

phr: Project Narrative Cannabis involvement is heritable and is more common in those with psychosis-related behaviors and schizophrenia, but the contributions to this co-occurrence remain unclear. title: Age-related differences in neurobiological systems supporting emotion
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Emotional experience shows well documented shifts across adulthood. application_id: 11179475
activity_code: DP1
title: High-dimensional chemical targeting of multiple neuron types to regulate energy balance
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
This high-risk, high-reward proposal will test whether we can use novel machine-learning methods to design
drug mixtures that jointly activate or inhibit, respectively, the activity of neurons that increase or decrease satiety,
while sparing off-target neurons types to reduce side effects. We will optimize this approach using cutting-edge
imaging of 1000s of hypothalamic neurons that control energy balance, and then test customized optimized
mixtures in mouse models of obesity. In future, we hope to use this approach to guide ongoing polypharmacy
approaches to treating diabetes and obesity in humans.

application_id: 11187113
activity_code: K99
title: Metabolic regulation of pancreatic metaplasia and neoplasia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Acinar-to-ductal metaplasia and pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia are precancerous lesions
and risk factors for the development of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. This proposal
addresses: 1. how metabolic changes in cellular redox pathways drive precancer and 2. how the
metabolic microenvironment regulates aspects of cell competition during metaplastic
transformation. Insights from the proposed experiments will connect major metabolic pathways
with precancerous states and inform new insights into pancreatic disease.

application_id: 11249467
activity_code: K23
title: In-hospital use of Automated Insulin Delivery (AID): Current state and future potential
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Hospitalized patients with diabetes face increased risks of morbidity and mortality due to high and low glycemic excursions during acute illness. The current standard of administering multiple daily injection insulin therapy to manage diabetes in the hospital is often inadequate and results in unacceptably high rates of both hyperglycemia and iatrogenic hypoglycemia. This proposal aims to examine and optimize the use of automated insulin delivery systems in the hospital by evaluating their effectiveness, identifying barriers and facilitators to their use in the hospital, and developing implementation strategies to improve glycemic control for hospitalized patients with diabetes.

application_id: 11181505
activity_code: K99
title: Validation and Kinetic Characterization of Blood-Cerebrospinal Fluid Barrier-Traversing Proteins in Aging With Library-Free Multiplexed Targeted Proteomics
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 It is known that blood-brain barrier function declines with aging, but the effects of aging on the choroid plexus,
a key component of the blood-brain barrier that transports molecules between the blood and cerebrospinal fluid,
are largely unknown. Measuring the extent to which blood-to-brain protein transport in the choroid plexus is
altered in aging would reveal which transporters are best targeted as drivers of brain aging or as drug delivery
vehicles. This proposal describes using novel mass spectrometry-based proteomic technologies to measure how
the kinetics of translocation of transporters and cargo in the choroid plexus change with aging.

application_id: 10906906
activity_code: K23
title: Deficits of the Early Visual System in Schizophrenia, a Combined Psychophysical, Computational, and Neuroimaging Approach
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: NARRATIVE Schizophrenia is a chronic and disabling psychiatric disorder, with modestly effective available treatments, and poorly understood brain mechanisms. Since the visual system is one of the most extensively studied neural systems in basic neuroscience, this study aims to understand the neural mechanisms of the visual impairments in schizophrenia, through behavioral experiments, computer simulations of the neural networks, and neuroimaging. Such a mechanistic understanding of schizophrenia could lead to novel targets for therapeutic interventions and potentially yield objective biomarkers of response to treatment and facilitate early detection strategies.

application_id: 11175251
activity_code: F30
title: Contribution of hydrogen sulfide to Duchenne muscular dystrophy pathophysiology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Defects in hydrogen sulfide (H2S) metabolism have been implicated in dystrophin-deficient muscular dystrophy
(namely, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, DMD), but the pathomechanism is not well understood. This proposal
will elucidate the contribution(s) of chronic, systemic H2S supplementation to in vivo contractility, including
protection from eccentric contraction-induced force loss, in the mdx mouse model of DMD in addition to defining
the effects of low H2S levels on oxidative post-translational cysteine in mdx contractile proteins. This study will
thus provide further insight into the mechanisms of contractile dysfunction in dystrophin-deficient muscular
dystrophies, allowing for the generation of improved DMD therapeutics.

title: Integrating genomic and spatial insights on Mtb transmission into policy-relevant models
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Community-based interventions to interrupt mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) transmission are needed to
reduce the considerable global burden of TB, but evidence on how to spatially target these interventions for
increased feasibility and efficiency is lacking. I propose to add to the evidence base by (1) developing a tool to
estimate the subnational burden of TB by integrating programmatic, mobility, and survey data from Uganda; (2)
characterizing transmission within and across distinct but linked communities using genomic data from 3
studies in Uganda and South Africa; and (3) projecting the impact and cost-effectiveness of TB prevention and
case finding interventions by fitting a mathematical model of Mtb transmission to data from a large study in
South Africa. Combined...

org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative TANK-binding kinase 1 (TBK1), a critical downstream effector of mutant active KRAS, is central to Axl- driven epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) in KRAS-mutant Pancreatic Cancer (PDA); however, the mechanism of how TBK1 drives EMT has yet to be elucidated. I hypothesize that Axl-mediated TBK1 drives EMT via direct activation of AKT3 and the stability of downstream transcriptional networks. application_id: 11170396
activity_code: K24
title: Brain and Behavioral Mechanisms Linking Loneliness and Social Isolation with Accelerated Cognitive Aging and Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Loneliness and social isolation are strongly linked to indices of healthy brain aging, cognitive decline, and
dementia. Reducing loneliness/isolation is a potent strategy to promote healthy brain aging and prevent
cognitive decline and Alzheimer’s Disease and Related Dementias (ADRD). This application forms the basis
for an evolving program of research on the translational science of loneliness/isolation and its role in brain
aging, with the long-term objective to identify and disseminate evidence-based interventions for
loneliness/isolation in later life, including determining the mechanisms that account for improved health
outcomes—mental health, cognitive health, and physical health outcomes.

activity_code: F31
title: Characterizing the impact of maternal immune response to urinary tract infection on preterm birth
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Preterm birth is the leading cause of mortality in infants under age one, resulting in over one million neonatal deaths annually and increased risk of infection, neurodevelopmental disorders, and cardiovascular and pulmonary disease in surviving preterm infants. The objective of this proposal is to define the impact of maternal urinary tract infection (UTI) on preterm birth incidence and averse neonatal outcomes through comprehensive characterization of the bladder, systemic, and reproductive immune response. The success of this work will illuminate potential therapeutic opportunities to predict, reduce, or prevent UTI-associated preterm birth.

application_id: 11166302
activity_code: K99
title: Unraveling the Role of p53 in Endothelium Homeostasis after Ionizing Radiation Exposure
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The current project is designed to study radiation-induced coronary heart disease (RICHD), a major concern in
cancer survivors receiving radiation therapy. With miniaturized human vessel tissues and novel mouse models,
I will map how radiation exposure dysregulates the vascular health over time and correlate molecular changes
to specific mutations in TP53 gene. The ultimate goal is to provide insights for the stratification of cancer patients
based on their genetic risk for RICHD to improve patient outcomes post-radiation therapy.

activity_code: F31
title: Molecular Origins of Phenotypic Changes in the Obese Microvascular Endothelium
org_name: AUGUSTA UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The studies proposed in this application will examine the landscape of microvascular endothelial cell phenotypes in obesity as well as investigate the role of HDAC9 in microvascular NOX1 expression and endothelial dysfunction in obesity. We will employ a novel AAV model of hyperphagia and obesity that can be easily combined with genetic models of endothelial lineage tracing and endothelial HDAC9 deletion to test the hypothesis that obesity upregulates HDAC9 to alter endothelial cell heterogeneity to favor loss of function, inflammatory phenotypes expressing NOX1. These studies will identify new mechanisms and paradigms by which endothelial dysfunction develops in obesity which may guide future therapeutic strategies to improve cardiovascular health.

title: Managing Excess Infant Energy Intake by Increasing Satiation Responsiveness in Dyadic Feeding Interactions
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: NARRATIVE Emerging evidence suggests that infants with greater appetitive drive may benefit from different feeding approaches for the prevention of obesity and chronic disease, including slowing the rate of milk intake and improving satiation signaling. application_id: 11075249
activity_code: K01
title: PFAS and Diabetic Kidney Disease in Young Onset Type 2 Diabetes: Emerging Risk Factors and Underlying Mechanisms
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Youth-onset type 2 diabetes significantly contributes to the increasing prevalence of diabetic kidney disease,
yet our understanding of modifiable risk factors remains limited. This study seeks to explore the role of
exposure to per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), an emerging environmental risk factor, in the onset
and progression of diabetic kidney disease among youth with type 2 diabetes by integrating data on PFAS
exposure with data on metabolites and proteins linked to uremic toxicity. This research project will enhance
understanding of the health impacts of PFAS, thereby supporting PFAS regulation and helping develop
innovative strategies for early identification and prevention of diabetic kidney disease.

application_id: 10989933
activity_code: K23
title: Quantitative interictal networks to localize seizure generators
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Current surgical options for intractable epilepsy result in continued seizures in 30-60% of cases. New research, including our own, suggests that quantitative analysis of the interictal EEG — time periods between seizures — can localize seizure generators and may help improve surgical planning. The goals of this study are to determine the spatiotemporal characteristics of two alternate quantitative methods of analyzing interictal EEG, to test whether these methods accurately localize seizure generators, and to determine if probing the interictal EEG with electrical stimulation improves localization.

application_id: 10820141
activity_code: F31
title: The Dynamics of DNA in Salmonella Persisters in Macrophages
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative Bacterial antibiotic persistence contributes to treatment failure, relapsing infections and selection of antibiotic resistance, yet mechanisms of persistence remain ill defined. In this project I will characterize properties and vulnerabilities of Salmonella persisters during infection to identify potential targets for their elimination. I will focus on the recent observation that chromosome accumulation confers a survival advantage to persisters and I aim at deciphering the benefit of such new genetic material.

application_id: 11192868
activity_code: K76
title: Estimating the treatment effect of cardiovascular medications to modify the risk for future cognitive decline in older adults
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Older adults frequently cite maintenance of cognitive function and prevention of dementia as their top health
priority. This is highly relevant to cardiovascular treatment decisions as the modifiable risk factors for cognitive
decline and cardiovascular disease overlap and early data suggest potentially differential treatment effects of
cardiovascular therapies on cognitive outcomes. We propose to evaluate the effect of two of the most
commonly prescribed cardiovascular therapies (Beta-blockers & Calcium Channel Blockers) on cognitive
trajectory and risk for Alzheimer’s Disease and Related Dementias among older adults by leveraging existing
longitudinal data from SPRINT MIND and prospective randomized trial data from the PCORI-sponsored
LIVEBETTER pragmatic trial.

activity_code: K23
title: Identification of Young Infants with Bronchiolitis at Low Risk of Developing Respiratory Progression
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Up to one-third of young infants aged ≤90 days old who present to the emergency department (ED) with viral bronchiolitis have potentially avoidable hospitalizations, contributing to preventable exposure to hospital- acquired infections, undue caregiver distress, and high healthcare costs. An accurate clinical prediction model is needed to identify young infants at low risk of developing respiratory progression who can be safely discharged to home. Using clinical history and physical examination factors, identification of the etiologic viruses, and physiologic time series data measured from bedside monitors, the proposed study will create new prediction models to guide clinical decisions and improve clinical outcomes for young infants with bronchiolitis.

title: Spinal Cord Injury: A Cardiovascular Perspective using Ultrasound
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The acute cardiovascular care of spinal cord injury, including blood pressure regulation, is not backed by sufficient evidence to form hard guidelines. title: 3D Culture Models of Partial-EMT and Its Regulation in Oral Cavity Squamous Cell Carcinoma
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Head and neck cancers are the sixth leading cause of cancer death worldwide, in part due to major challenges
in patient stratification that have prevented the use of novel targeted therapeutics. The proposed work seeks to
utilize three-dimensional (3D) culture models of oral cancers, the most common subsite of head and neck cancer,
to better understanding of the biology associated with aggressive disease, as well as tumor features that may
predict response to both standard and novel targeted treatments. We believe this approach is applicable beyond
oral cancers, and the proposed work may support a paradigm shift toward using such 3D culture models to
predict patient response to novel targeted therapies, particularly in the recurrent or metastatic setting.

application_id: 10828893
activity_code: K08
title: Lung Myofibroblast De-Differentiation and Fibrosis Resolution Depend on cAMP-mediated Inhibition of HuR.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) is a common and deadly scarring lung disease for which there is no cure. The development and progression of IPF proceeds when myofibroblasts – scar forming and contractile cells that differentiate from fibroblasts – fail to de-differentiate following lung injury. This proposal seeks to investigate the mechanisms by which myofibroblasts can be de-differentiated and cleared – thereby enabling fibrosis resolution – as well as to support the career development of a promising and committed physician scientist.

title: Investigating the epidermal microenvironment in melanoblast migration and invasion: a novel approach to understanding invasive melanoma
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative Invasive melanoma is a potentially deadly medical condition, with an incidence of 100,000 new cases in 2021 alone. Melanoma research has mainly focused on melanocyte dysregulation to understand the disease; yet, the epidermis is an important microenvironment for healthy melanocytes, which adhere to and communicate with keratinocytes to maintain homeostasis. In this project, we will test hypothesis that melanocytes and epidermal cells form specialized adhesions important for melanocyte migration and retention within the epidermis, thereby contributing to the suppression of melanoma metastasis.

title: Neurocomputational substrates of maladaptive uncertainty learning and avoidance in anxiety
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Anxiety disorders are characterized by problems learning about potentially dangerous situations, particularly when possible outcomes in a situation are uncertain. The present application proposes to use neural and computational approaches to understand why people high in anxiety have difficulties learning about uncertainty and why uncertain situations are avoided in and outside of the laboratory. The goal of this application is to determine what learning difficulties are present in anxiety in order to develop more effective treatments that target these learning difficulties.

title: Transcriptional variation in human milk
org_name: J. DAVID GLADSTONE INSTITUTES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Human milk provides significant health benefits to infants as well as the lactating parent, and while breastfeeding is recommended for the first six months of life, many families struggle to initiate and maintain lactation. Few clinical interventions exist to improve lactation outcomes, in part due to lack of understanding of the cellular process of lactation by cells in the mammary gland. This study will leverage existing transcriptomic data of mammary gland cells from human milk to identify the gene pathways underlying the process of lactation and its response to disease.

application_id: 11134679
activity_code: K08
title: In vivo enrichment of cell-free DNA to boost sensitivity of circulating nucleic-acid based diagnostics
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) tests can detect diseases like cancer earlier and with just a blood draw, but current tests
have low sensitivity for detecting microscopic levels of disease in the body. If we can recover from cfDNA from
patient samples, we could significantly boost the sensitivity of current cfDNA tests. This proposal will pursue a
novel way to achieve this through engineering of novel antibodies that can protect and improve of the recovery
of cfDNA in the body.

title: Drug-drug interactions and kidney disease in hospitalized patients
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Drug-drug interactions (DDIs) are a growing source of preventable harm in patients with kidney disease. The sources of potential harm from DDIs in this population are bidirectional in nature, with kidney disease functioning as both an outcome of DDIs and a factor that alters DDI severity. The goal of this proposal is to understand and quantify these bidirectional relationships in order to improve the safety of pharmacotherapy in this vulnerable population.

activity_code: F31
title: Cytoskeleton-mediated regulation of insulin secretion hot spots in pancreatic beta cells
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Pancreatic β cells secrete insulin into the vasculature at specific locations called secretion hot spots, where various cytoskeletal and mechanosensitive proteins localize and modulate secretion levels. Since the mechanisms of this regulation are to a large extend elusive, I propose to determine details of organization of the cytoskeletal networks at hot spots and test if their molecular interplay regulates insulin secretion toward the vasculature. Understanding these molecular mechanisms may have high impact by providing potential targets for restoring normal glucose-stimulated insulin secretion in type 2 diabetes.

title: Corticofugal contributions to auditory perceptual learning

title: Defining the regulation and regulatory mechanisms of TCF-1 in CD8+ T cell differentiation
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The transcription factor TCF-1 (encoded by Tcf7) is required for the development of acute infection-induced central memory T cells (Tcm) and chronic infection-induced T progenitor exhausted cells (TPEX) that mediate durable CD8 T cell responses. Although it is known that Tcf7 is downregulated by pro-inflammatory cytokines during the terminal differentiation of CD8 T cells, it is unclear how cell-extrinsic signals downregulate Tcf7 expression. activity_code: F32
title: Comparison of direct and indirect magnetic resonance imaging of myelin in Alzheimer's disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The goal of this project is to compare and evaluate the 3D STAIR-UTE direct myelin imaging technique and the
SyMRI indirect myelin imaging technique for evaluation of myelin damage in AD. We will conduct studies using
biological myelin phantoms, human brain specimens from donors without and with AD, and a cross-sectional
study of healthy volunteers and patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and AD, to validate novel
quantitative MRI techniques for detection of myelin quantity and quality. This fellowship will conduct a systematic
comparison between 3D STAIR-UTE and SyMRI in depicting myelin changes for both ex vivo samples and in
vivo controls and patients, thereby facilitating advancement of new diagnostic approaches for AD.

title: Epicardial Adipose Tissue and Cardiometabolic Risk Factors in Youth-Onset Type 2 Diabetes Undergoing Bariatric Surgery
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Epicardial adipose tissue has been suggested as a new cardiometabolic risk factor due to its anatomical proximity to the heart and coronary circulation and appears to be higher in adults with type 2 diabetes. Our lab utilizes novel, state-of-the-art MRI methods to assess cardiac structure and function, including specific fat depots. The proposed research will determine the relationship between epicardial adipose tissue and cardiometabolic health in youth with type 2 diabetes and investigate the impact of bariatric surgery on epicardial adipose tissue and cardiometabolic risk factors, independent of overall weight loss.

org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: NARRATIVE
Patients with disproportional jaws have underbites or open bites and suffer from speech disorders twenty times
more than the general public, whereby these patients seek jaw surgery and braces to treat issues heavily
impacting their quality of life, including abnormal chewing, facial esthetics, and speech. However, it is unclear
why these patients present with a high prevalence of articulation issues and whether surgically correcting their
jaws will improve their speech problems; as such, we as providers cannot provide evidence-based treatment
recommendations. To address this gap in knowledge, we will use state-of-the-art technologies to longitudinally
track speech changes pre- and post-operatively to evaluate outcomes of treatment and generate evidence-

application_id: 11180051
activity_code: F99
title: Investigating the susceptibility of basal forebrain cholinergic neurons in individuals with Down syndrome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Basal forebrain cholinergic neurons (BFCNs) are important for arousal, attention, learning, and memory, and
they are prone to degeneration in Down syndrome (DS) and other neurodegenerative diseases. The F99
proposal will determine if there are fewer BFCNs at birth in DS and utilize iPSC models while the K00 proposal
will expand this work to determine the temporal order of pathology accumulation in the DS basal forebrain and
identify molecular mechanisms that may contribute to BFCN susceptibility. A better understanding of the role of
BFCNs in disease progression may improve DS and other neurodegenerative disease models as well as
elucidate novel therapeutic targets.

title: Estrogen receptor regulation of cocaine effects on dopamine terminals
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: In recent years, understanding the neurobiological basis for substance use disorder (SUD) has been on the forefront of scientific agendas, which has elucidated sex as a significant biological variable in vulnerability to addiction. Females represent a particularly vulnerable population in developing cocaine use disorder and circulating gonadal hormones have been elucidated as key regulators of the dopamine system, drug responses, and motivated behavior associated with SUD. Defining the factors that underlie female-specific drug responses are critical to effectively treating them; however we must first elucidate how these sex differences fit into the comprehensive framework of dopamine regulation.

title: Neurogenetic contributions to the spread of tau pathology in sporadic early-onset Alzheimer's disease
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Early-onset Alzheimer’s disease (EOAD) is a critically understudied form of AD compared with its late-onset counterpart, with little evidence regarding the mechanisms underlying disease progression. title: Elucidating the Role of Neural Activity in Midbrain Dopamine Neuron Degeneration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Parkinson’s Disease (PD) is a neurodegenerative disease characterized by the loss of substantia nigra dopamine neurons, but our understanding of how their activity state contributes to their selective vulnerability is lacking. title: Opening small packages: unraveling roles for microproteins during early vertebrate development
org_name: STOWERS INSTITUTE FOR MEDICAL RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative Humans begin as fertilized eggs that coordinate the transformation into our familiar forms with biomolecules called proteins. Malfunctioning proteins can consequently result in birth defects, and emerging data has revealed many novel small proteins with the potential to affect human development. This study will discover the roles of these small proteins in development using an innovative system that targets and destroys protein making templates in the developmentally informative model zebrafish towards a better understanding of the players that cause human birth defects and diseases with developmental origins.

application_id: 11176979
activity_code: K99
title: Defining Roles of Perivascular Retinal Neurons by Integrating Visual Stimuli and Vascular Tones
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 My recent work led to a discovery of perivascular retinal neurons, termed Fam19a4/Nts-RGCs, that
selectively interact with vessels via perivascular endfeet. I further found that Piezo2, a mechanosensitive cation
channel, is enriched in these perivascular neurons at the interface of neurovascular interactions. My proposed
project will combine electrophysiology, experimental optic neuropathy models, single-cell profiling, and in vivo
imaging to investigate Piezo2's role in modulating these newly characterized perivascular neurons' physiological
properties and pathological responses.

activity_code: K23
title: Enhancing early care and education through reflective supervision to promote adaptive emotion regulation in young children
org_name: EMMA PENDLETON BRADLEY HOSPITAL
phr: NARRATIVE Emotion regulation (ER) problems are prevalent in young children living in poverty, and while high quality early care and education (ECE) programs are positioned to prevent child ER difficulties, they often fail to do so because teachers lack internal support to help them sustain evidence-based teaching practices and skills. Training ECE directors to provide enhanced supervision to their teachers may promote positive effects of ECE programming and adaptive ER in children. This study aims to refine and then test an intervention focused on training and supporting ECE directors in Reflective Practice and Supervision to dually target teaching and child outcomes.

activity_code: K23
title: Integrative Informatics Approach to Predict Readmissions and Improve Outcomes in COPD
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT BUFFALO
phr: Project Narrative
Patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease who are discharged from the hospital have high rates of
early readmission; these readmissions are a significant burden to the health care system. The proposed research
will combine social information with rich clinical data to develop a real-time readmission risk prediction algorithm
that will be integrated into a patient-centric intervention and tested in real-world clinical practice settings. The
information learned from this project will assist in providing individualized treatment at the transition from hospital
to home and focused on reducing healthcare utilization and ultimately improving quality of care.

application_id: 10873672
activity_code: F30
title: Non-Invasive Carotid Artery Measurements for Continuous Intracranial Pressure Monitoring
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Continuous intracranial pressure (ICP) monitoring is considered one of the most important surveillance tools for neurological and neurosurgical patients that can provide critical insights on disease severity to guide medical and surgical management. However, the standard for continuous ICP monitoring, which entails drilling a burr hole into the skull and implanting an intracranial catheter or pressure sensor, is far too often not practiced due to its invasiveness and risks for infection, hemorrhage, and dislodgement. Continuous, non-invasive monitoring of the hemodynamic waveform at the carotid artery using soft, conformal sensors shows promise as an indirect means of continuously and non-invasively monitoring ICP.

title: The Role of Reproductive Aging for Racial Differences in AD/ADRD from Midlife to Older Age
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed project will comprehensively assess the contribution of early menopause (natural and surgical) to overall risk and Black-White disparities in AD/ADRD diagnoses (adjudicated-AD/ADRD) and development (cognitive decline and neuroimaging markers of AD/ADRD) by harmonizing data from 25,051 women (30% Black) in three cohorts spanning midlife to older age. application_id: 10983858
activity_code: K08
title: Machine-learning prediction model for personalized urinary tract infection care in children
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Unlike in adults, febrile urinary tract infection in children can be a silent and dangerous condition because it is challenging to diagnose with potential risk of progression to severe infection and permanent loss of kidney function. This proposal seeks to minimize the risk by implementing a practical, validated machine-learning algorithm to promptly identify unsafe anatomy before injury occurs. The result of this proposed machine- learning implementation is expected to open the horizon and be scaled to other institutions, ultimately facilitating interactive improvements in the algorithm and decision support suitable for wide adoption in diverse contexts.

application_id: 10984412
activity_code: K23
title: Longitudinal Assessment of Distinct Motor Learning Processes to Inform Mechanistic Models of Sensorimotor Function in Psychosis
org_name: TRUSTEES OF INDIANA UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE. Sensorimotor disturbances have long been identified in psychotic disorders and may serve as objectively measurable readouts of fundamental process disturbances that drive clinical phenomenology. Determining which metrics of a motor learning task capture this dysfunction and fluctuate over time with symptoms is critical in linking aberrant behavior processes to the disruption of distinct neural circuits and resulting clinical symptoms. Enriching our models of brain-behavior-symptom relationships can then be leveraged to identify and develop targeted interventions for psychosis onset and maintenance.

title: Leveraging psychobiologic markers of stress to reduce severe maternal morbidity in pregnant people with mental illness
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The rising prevalence of mental illness and its significant contribution to maternal morbidity and
mortality necessitate the development of feasible, scalable, and effective interventions. This
proposal is a first step towards addressing this problem by focusing on reducing the risk of
severe maternal morbidity (SMM), which precedes most maternal deaths, in people with mental

innovative and impactful research in maternal and child health for years to come.

title: Developing and Testing a Multilevel, Culturally Appropriate Lifestyle Intervention For Hispanic Patients with Metabolic-dysfunction Associated Steatotic Liver Disease

phr: NARRATIVE Metabolic-dysfunction associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) is a highly prevalent liver complication that disproportionately effects Hispanic/Latino patients. This study will develop and test the feasibility of a culturally appropriate, multilevel lifestyle intervention to provide MASLD-specific education and support while also addressing the influences of family, healthcare providers and access to social services on lifestyle behaviors and weight management in Hispanic/Latino patients with MASLD.

phr: Project Narrative  This K01 application describes a research and mentoring strategy for Dr. Su Chin Heo to gain the necessary biological skill set to identify how spatio-temporal genome organization regulates gene expression in meniscus cells. How genome organization of cells within the knee meniscus change with development or degeneration is not well understood, and how biophysical cues impact this process has not been studied. application_id: 10873837
activity_code: K01
title: Quantifying the Mechanism and Impact of Hip Extensor Muscle Dysfunction During Aerobic Exercise in Hip Osteoarthritis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: Project Narrative Exercise-based programs for hip osteoarthritis (OA) lack scientific evidence and are ineffective in conservatively treating hip OA. Current experimental protocols are unable to precisely assess the underlying pathomechanism of hip extensor dysfunction that is associated with abnormal hip joint mechanics, hip pain and cartilage breakdown during exercise in hip OA. This study will lead to the development of a novel, multi-modal experimental platform, consisting of magnetic resonance imaging, muscle-function assessment, musculoskeletal simulations and biochemical biomarkers, which will be used to assess the pathomechanism and corresponding effects of hip extensor dysfunction during aerobic exercise in patients with symptomatic hip OA.

title: A novel method to identify regulators of biological aging  based on high-throughput sequencing of epigenetic clocks.
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Developing accurate, cost-effective, and scalable biomarkers of aging and age-related frailty could help in assessing longevity interventions in the lab or patient outcomes in the context of clinical trials of age-related disease. This proposal describes the development of a method for high-throughput assay of DNA methylation clocks, a robust class of age predictors, which will be used to make a novel type of biomarker based on age and mouse frailty scores. title: Pilot Implementation of Measurement-Based Care in Community Opioid Treatment Programs
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 New England is one of the epicenters of the opioid overdose crisis, with five New England states
ranking in the top 10 with the highest overdose deaths. This mentored research proposal will evaluate the
preliminary effectiveness and implementation of measurement-based care (MBC) as a treatment progress

activity_code: K01
title: Inducing Mirth to Improve Memory in Depression
org_name: MCLEAN HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) is characterized by poor memory for positive events. This positive memory deficit exacerbates the hopelessness that characterizes MDD, and it may be a critical target for treatment—but, unfortunately, the underlying mechanisms are poorly understood. The current project will address this knowledge gap by using electroencephalography (EEG) and computational modeling to study the encoding and retrieval of positive memories in adults with MDD, and by testing whether mirth induction can alleviate the deficit by increasing positive affect and thus restoring neural responses to positive stimuli.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Family caregivers often act as an extension of the care team during home health care for older adults, yet their capacity and needs are not systematically assessed which limits their access to necessary support. This proposal would develop, refine, and pilot test a self-assessment intervention for the home health setting to improve communication and support between the care team and family caregivers to patients with Alzheimer’s Disease and Related Dementias. Proposed research and training activities would support the career development of Dr. Julia Burgdorf, whose career goal is to improve home- and community-based care for older adults by developing pragmatic clinical interventions to advance provider-led engagement and support of family caregivers.

title: A combined epidemiologic and genomic approach to identify and control transmission events of hospital associated infections
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Antibiotic-resistant bacteria are responsible for over 2.8 million infections and 35,000 deaths each year in the United States. This proposal aims to develop a method that integrates multiple data sources to determine how pathogens are spreading within a healthcare facility. This will provide improved evidence that is necessary to target key steps on the transmission pathway and implement more targeted measures to control healthcare- associated pathogens.

title: Clinical study to evaluate the methylation signature of head and neck squamous cell carcinoma pain
org_name: LOMA LINDA UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Head and neck cancer is one of the most painful malignancies, and is woefully unresponsive to even high doses of opioids. This study will 1) establish the relationship between pain experienced by head and neck cancer patients and specific genes that are silenced by methylation, 2) therapeutically target identified genes using a preclinical model, and 3) provide training to further the career development of the applicant as a surgeon scientist. The study has the potential to lead to a non-opioid treatment for head and neck cancer pain.

org_name: NORTHEASTERN UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Young sexual and gender minorities who have sex with men (YSGMSM) carry a disproportionate burden of the HIV epidemic in the United States, yet Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP) uptake and adherence has been inadequate for these groups despite evidence that it is safe and effective at reducing HIV transmission for YSGMSM. Syndemics theory is used to characterize convergent and mutually reinforcing social (e.g., race), structural (e.g., neighborhood poverty), and digital (e.g., social media consumption, access to internet) factors impacting HIV risk in YSGMSM. This proposal incorporates this syndemics approach into iTech’s P3 study, an NICHD-funded RCT to improve PrEP adherence among YSGMSM, to understand how convergent digital and neighborhood factors impact PrEP adherence ...

application_id: 11367855
activity_code: K12
title: Pediatric Scientist Development Program
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative
Advancement in child health is threatened by the decline and limited diversity of pediatrician-scientists in the
biomedical workforce. The Pediatric Scientist Development Program (PSDP) has implemented a
comprehensive strategy to expand the boundaries of child health research and develop the research careers of
rigorously trained, diverse pediatric physician-scientists. The PSDP's primary goal is to produce the next
generation of diverse investigative pediatricians who will discover the causes and cures of childhood illnesses,
recognizing that a diverse group of pediatrician physician-scientists is better equipped to address health issues
affecting all communities and achieve maximum health impact.

title: A multi-omics approach to evaluate the role of chronic inflammation in breast cancer development
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative This Pathway to Independence Award will apply a multi-omics approach, building on the unique design and rich dietary and biomarker data in the Nurses’ Health Studies to comprehensively evaluate the relationship between chronic inflammation and breast cancer risk. application_id: 11166276
activity_code: F31
title: Development and application of whole-body patient-specific computational mesh phantoms for organ dosimetry and second primary cancer risk quantification following external beam radiotherapy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
External beam radiotherapy is an effective and widely used approach for the curative treatment of cancer.
Increasing epidemiological evidence suggests that radiation-induced second primary cancers are a significant
risk following radiotherapy, and therefore should be considered in the treatment planning and dose optimization
processes for both near- and out-of-field organs. The proposed study will develop an end-to-end system for
patient-specific dosimetry and cancer risk quantification for all normal organs following radiotherapy, with a focus
on children and young adults.

title: NXF2-mediated RNA transport in the male germline
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
By studying how loss of NXF2 leads to male infertility and discovering if the NXF2 protein has gained a novel

hypothesis is correct, I will increase our understanding of testicular germ cell biology through discovering a

testicular germ cells. My studies of mouse NXF2 in fertility defects will help inform cases of human male infertility
associated with NXF2 mutations.

title: Adapting and evaluating an integrated intervention for adolescent substance use and pain during oral surgery
org_name: BROWN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Substance use among adolescents (ADOL) is prevalent, yet grossly undertreated. Wisdom tooth extraction is a
common, acutely painful procedure for ADOL that represents an opportune time to identify ADOL substance
use and engage ADOL in brief intervention due to shared risk factors between SU and pain, the potential for

increased risk for post-operative complications. This study aims to adapt and evaluate a novel, scalable pre-
surgical brief intervention for ADOL with substance use undergoing oral surgery (i.e. wisdom tooth extraction)

application_id: 10813082
activity_code: K99
title: K+ channel structural dynamics landscape: from selectivity to gating
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE K+ channels are membrane proteins that allow rapid and exquisite selective flow of K+ ions across the cell membrane that is crucial to generate chemical and electrical activities in living cells, and their dysfunction lead to many human diseases. To completely understand the behavior of ion channels, dynamic views of the structures are essential to fill the gap between crystal/CryoEM structures and single ion channel current analyses. The structural dynamics of potassium channel proteins presents an exciting and emerging field of study which will provide new understanding of the fundamental basis of channel function.

application_id: 11141687
activity_code: K23
title: Clinical and biologic risk factors for post-tuberculosis lung disease (PTLD) in people with and without HIV
org_name: TULANE UNIVERSITY OF LOUISIANA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Post-tuberculosis lung disease (PTLD), which develops in up to half of people with pulmonary
tuberculosis, is an increasingly recognized cause of lung disease with up to 4.3 million new cases
worldwide every year. However, the underlying biologic mechanisms that lead to PTLD are still poorly
understood. This proposal aims to understand the underlying clinical host factors and host immunological
factors that lead to PTLD, measuring lung function over two-years after pulmonary tuberculosis and
using state-of-the art techniques including RNA sequencing.

application_id: 11097352
activity_code: K01
title: The role of maladaptive VEGFR2 signaling in renal stroma for chronic kidney disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Acute kidney injury is a common kidney disease, often found in critically ill patients, which is induced such as by
sudden cardiac arrest or by side effects from a chemotherapeutic agent. A dire consequence of acute kidney
injury is progression to chronic kidney disease, which accounts for approximately 6.7% of all Medicare
expenditure. This study will combine the use of cultured kidney cells with genetically modified mouse models
and pharmacological approaches to investigate whether a gene termed VEGF-R2 plays an important role in
acute kidney injury to chronic kidney disease progression.

title: Guideline to Implementation: A Rapid Clinical Care Pathway to Care for Patients Affected by Chronic Constipation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chronic constipation affects 10-20% of the population and leads to 2 million gastroenterology referrals each year in the US. We will apply behavioral economics, user-centered design, and implementation science to develop a novel clinical care pathway to rapidly triage patients to effective and targeted therapy for constipation using a recently validated point-of-care diagnostic device. This work will allow more patients to receive efficient and appropriate care and will help patients avoid unnecessary tests and repeated trials of ineffective and costly laxatives.

title: Viper-active: Snake Venom Microbiome as a Source for Bacterially-derived Molecule Discovery
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
Animal venoms are a well-established source of bioactive molecules, which have been historically beneficial to
human health through pharmacological drug discovery as well as harmful during snakebite envenomation. Few
studies have investigated the metabolism and function of venom-associated microbes and their infectious and
bioactive potential. This proposal combines field and laboratory studies with microbial multi-omic and molecular
bioassay techniques to understand the breadth of bacterial diversity in snake venom, identify bioactive bacterially
produced compounds, and elucidate their role in venom toxicity.

activity_code: DP5
title: Novel Functional Anatomic and Biomarker Indices of Radiation-Induced Female Sexual Toxicities in a Multi-Center Cohort
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative
The objective of this study is to define novel dosimetric predictors of female sexual dysfunction and to identify
quantitative imaging and microbiome-based biomarker indices associated with radiation damage to specific
sexual organs using a classification of sexual dysfunction structured on functional anatomic domains
(dermatologic, vaginal and erectile). Towards this end, we will conduct an observational prospective cohort study
of 110 female patients treated with pelvic radiation at institutions in New York City, Atlanta, and Houston. Our
results will rapidly provide data and inform innovative, personalized interventions to preserve female sexual
function or mitigate the effects of radiation in this understudied population.

activity_code: F31
title: Toward therapeutic targeting of liquid-liquid phase separation dynamics in skin
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Liquid-liquid phase separation recently emerged as a novel driver of skin barrier formation. Mechanistically, the underlying intracellular phase separation dynamics govern the assembly and disassembly of enigmatic keratohyalin granules, which are hallmarks of epidermal differentiation conspicuously absent in human skin barrier disorders. Deploying innovative biomolecular engineering approaches in human epidermal equivalent models, this proposal will enable the molecular-level characterization and design of functional keratohyalin granules, laying a foundation for future therapeutic manipulation of epidermal phase separation dynamics.
