application_id: 11137658
activity_code: K08
title: Investigating mechanosensation in the glaucomatous optic nerve head
org_name: UPSTATE MEDICAL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Glaucoma, a leading cause of irreversible blindness worldwide, is projected to affect 100 million
individuals by the year 2040. It is defined as an age-associated optic neuropathy characterized
by retinal ganglion cell death; a barrier to treating glaucoma is that the mechanisms underlying
cell susceptibility in this disease are very poorly understood. This proposal investigates
mechanisms by which astrocytes, the primary support cells for retinal ganglion cells, sense their
mechanical environment in glaucoma; the data obtained from these studies will help to develop
novel therapies to treat this difficult disease.

activity_code: K08
title: Cannabis Toxicology in Relation to Neurocognitive Outcomes in Adolescents
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
While personal cannabis use and secondhand cannabis smoke exposure is prevalent among youth, the
influence of these on adolescent cognitive development remains understudied. Cannabis product complexity
and limitations of self-report may obscure important relationships between delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC)
and neurocognitive development/outcomes. Here we propose using quantifiable toxicological information of
personal and secondhand cannabis exposure from hair to predict cognitive functioning over time in
adolescents.

application_id: 11170432
activity_code: DP2
title: Defining the role of single-cell heterogeneity in bacterial vaginosis
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite extensive characterization, we struggle to understand and control the progression of human-associated
microbial communities, including bacterial vaginosis (BV), a condition that is directly impactful to women’s health
and the acquisition of sexually transmitted infections. This proposal investigates single-cell genomic and
transcriptomic heterogeneity as two underexplored areas that impact the progression of BV. This intellectually
and methodologically innovative research will transform our understanding of the development, treatment, and
recurrence of BV and will provide broad-reaching insights into new strategies to prevent and treat polymicrobial
infections.

title: Using high dimensional molecular data to decipher gene dynamics underlying pathogenic synovial fibroblasts

application_id: 10907644
activity_code: K08
title: Investigating the Downstream Mechanisms Contributing to Beta Cell Failure in Offspring Exposed to Maternal Obesity
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: Project Narrative: Children born to obese mothers are at increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes at a younger age and preclinical models demonstrate the presence of worsened pancreatic beta cell dysfunction in males when compared to female offspring, but the mechanistic pathways underlying this observation are unclear. The goal of this proposal is to delineate the role of Reg3g-mediated heparan sulfate polymerization as a mechanistic pathway amenable to treatment of sex-specific islet dysfunction in offspring of obese mice. This proposal will address a critical gap in our understanding of the developmental programming of offspring islet dysfunction induced by maternal obesity and has the potential to inform novel therapeutic strategies.

application_id: 10984468
activity_code: K08
title: Regulators and interactomes of CaV1.2 in health and disease
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Heart failure is associated with changes in the surface expression and regulation of the Calcium channel CaV1.2, which contributes to the exertional limitations and propensity for arrhythmia experienced by patients with this disease. Combining APEX proximity labeling with quantitative TMT SPS MS3, I will map the changing “neighborhood” of CaV1.2 in chronic heart failure. Using genetic models of APEX-defined candidate proteins, cellular electrophysiology, and an innovative transgenic mouse model engineered to assess CaV1.2 surface expression, I will gain deep insights into the molecular and cellular pathophysiology of heart failure.

org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cardiovascular disease, the number one leading cause of death in the United States, is highly prevalent in cancer survivors. application_id: 11098497
activity_code: K08
title: NOT-gated CAR T cells to overcome on-target, off-tumor toxicity in AML
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Development of chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cell therapy for acute myeloid leukemia (AML) has been
limited in part by a lack of truly tumor-specific target antigens, which can render healthy tissues susceptible to
“on-target, off-tumor toxicity.” Engineering combinations of activating and inhibitory synthetic receptors into CAR
T cells will address this challenge by enhancing tumor specificity. Here, I will identify inhibitory motifs and critical
signaling mechanisms within CAR T cells that will contribute to the design of next-generation CAR T cells for
AML, with a specific focus on circumventing endothelial cell toxicity.

title: Multivariate Modeling of the Neural Mechanisms of Treatment Response in Opioid Addiction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The proposed K01 project will use multimodal magnetic resonance imaging and machine learning techniques to elucidate the structural and functional brain mechanisms underlying treatment failure in young adults receiving treatment with injectable extended-release naltrexone (XR-NTX) for opioid use disorder. title: Investigating a Role for Antigen Presentation by Enteric Glial Cells in Experimental Autoimmune Encephalomyelitis

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Multiple sclerosis (MS) is driven by an autoimmune attack against myelin in the Central Nervous System, though the etiology of this disease remains unclear. application_id: 11175463
activity_code: K01
title: Utilizing Implementation Research Methodologies to Adapt an Intervention to Reduce Alcohol Use and Improve HIV Care Outcomes among Men Living with HIV who have Serodiscordant Pregnant Partners
org_name: SAN DIEGO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hazardous alcohol use is prevalent among men living with HIV (MLWH) in Uganda and associated with non-
adherence to antiretroviral therapy (ART) and detectable viral load, which is especially problematic in
serodiscordant partnerships with pregnant partners as it threatens Uganda’s progress towards HIV elimination
goals. Therefore, intervening on hazardous alcohol use among MLWH with serodiscordant pregnant partners is
critical to both HIV prevention and treatment efforts. We apply the Exploration, Preparation, Implementation,
Sustainment (EPIS) framework to guide adaptation of an intervention with strong preliminary efficacy reducing
alcohol use and improving ART adherence among MLWH in Uganda for use specifically among MLWH with
serodiscordant pregnant partners.

application_id: 11158718
activity_code: K01
title: Brief Transdiagnostic Treatment for Anxiety Disorders and PTSD in South Africa: A Hybrid-Effectiveness Trial
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: NARRATIVE
The proposed study aims to conduct a hybrid effectiveness-implementation study, harnessing the strengths of
both quantitative and qualitative studies, to examine the effectiveness of a nurse-delivered brief transdiagnostic
treatment for anxiety disorders and PTSD in South African primary care clinics. The proposed study will also
examine the barriers and facilitators to the implementation of this treatment in primary care clinics in South Africa.
The findings from our study have implications for increasing access and the scale-up of evidence-based
treatments for anxiety disorders and PTSD in South Africa and other low and middle-income countries.

title: Caregivers-Patients Support to Cope with Advanced-Cancer
org_name: PONCE SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: The goal of this K08 is to prepare the PI (Normarie Torres-Blasco, Ph.D.) to become an independent investigator in Implementation and Dissemination Science in palliative care. The research goal of this proposal is to refine and pilot an intervention titled Caregivers-Patients Support for Advanced Cancer (CASA), comprising five 60-minute telehealth sessions for patient-caregiver dyads. This study aims to utilize a collaborative intervention planning framework that integrates community-based participatory research, dissemination, and implementation models to modify and assess the feasibility and acceptability of the CASA intervention.

application_id: 11180057
activity_code: F31
title: Digital phenotypes from a low-cost smartwatch to inform early detection of Alzheimer's disease and related dementias
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
As the aging US population increases and interventions for dementia become available for early stages, there
is a growing need for low-cost sensitive tools to detect early cognitive decline in large samples. Digital
phenotyping, a novel approach involving passive data collection from sensors on personal devices, such as
smartwatches, could fill this gap to estimate early autonomic dysfunction and health behaviors associated with
early cognitive decline. This project will examine the validity of several metrics from a commercially available
and low-cost smartwatch in a diverse sample of older adults, which could have broad implications for health
behavior monitoring, early detection of cognitive decline, and targeted interventions.

application_id: 11127546
activity_code: K01
title: Smartphone-based meditation training to reduce adolescent depression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed project seeks to reduce the burden of adolescent depression by evaluating in a randomized trial
an 8-week mobile health meditation intervention in adolescents reporting elevated depressive symptoms and
characterizing psychological, behavioral, and psychophysiological mechanisms of change using innovative
ambulatory assessment methods. Long-term, this program of research is designed to test and optimize the
feasibility, acceptability, safety, efficacy, and effectiveness of mobile technologies for the treatment and
prevention of adolescent depression. The current study will provide essential training and pilot data for a future
large-scale randomized clinical trial involving an active control arm and ambulatory assessment of candidate
mechanisms of change.

application_id: 11361347
activity_code: DP2
title: Compliant Limb Reconstruction: Co-engineering Body and Machine to Revolutionize Limb Salvage
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Injury or disease in the bones and joints can fundamentally alter the mechanical behavior of human limbs, leading
to painful, unnatural movements. In many cases, the state-of-the-art treatments that are intended to treat these
conditions can leave patients with a limb that is alive and attached to their body, but unable to support even basic
function. My approach combines new surgical techniques with innovative mechanical design to restore the
mechanical properties of damaged limbs, thereby eliminating joint pain, restoring function and quality of life, and
preventing unnecessary amputation.

application_id: 10841020
activity_code: K23
title: Reducing Disparities in Pediatric Diabetes: Building the Evidence Base to Inform Effective Diabetes Technology Interventions in Underrepresented Minorities
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Youth from low socioeconomic groups are at a systematic disadvantage in the provision of diabetes care, particularly diabetes technology which is associated with improvement in diabetes-specific outcomes. Thus, we urgently need studies to understand and ameliorate the persistent worsening of disparities of diabetes management and outcomes in youth from low socioeconomic backgrounds. This proposed research will (1) improve representation of diverse youth in the literature, (2) address the gap in knowledge of barriers and promoters in publicly insured youth, and (3) identify and address factors of worsening disparity in diabetes technology.

title: Synaptic plasticity underlying sex differences in aggression reward
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative Aggression is often considered a “negative” behavior, but proper display of aggression is essential for self- preservation and maintenance of resources. application_id: 11001142
activity_code: K01
title: Silent Zones of Lung Disease in COPD
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Project Narrative: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is diagnosed by spirometry but significant disagreement exists between spirometric diagnosis and presence of emphysema on computed tomography (CT) due to possibility that a substantial portion of the lung has abnormal mechanics. Using image registration to match inspiratory and expiratory scans point-to-point, we will identify Silent Zones of the lung that appear normal using density thresholds but have abnormal mechanics. The proposed project will use a combination of quantitative imaging and deep learning tools to establish a novel CT biomarker that will enhance diagnosis of clinically significant COPD and also identify individuals at high risk for disease progression.

activity_code: F32
title: Glycosaminoglycan-mediated fibril sliding and its role in fatigue-induced microdamage and rupture in aged and healing Achilles tendons
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
Musculoskeletal tissues like tendon undergo a variety of mechanical changes at the microscale that lead to
inferior tissue-level mechanics and increased rupture rates. The purpose of this study is to elucidate the role of
glycosaminoglycans in fatigue-induced rupture in aging and healing Achilles tendons using a multiscale
experimental and computational approach. This research will innovate current biomechanical evaluations of
tendon rupture due to fatigue, enhance our understanding of how tendon ruptures occur in vulnerable
populations, and inform future treatments to improve tendon function and prevent injury.

activity_code: F31
title: Modulation of Somatic Repeat Expansion as a Therapeutic Approach to Huntington's Disease
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Huntington's disease is a fatal neurodegenerative condition caused by a genetic defect that worsens over time
as a result of faulty DNA mismatch repair. This proposal seeks to use small RNA drugs to modulate key mediators
of mismatch repair in a mouse model of Huntington's disease, and determine the impact on blocking or reversing
the gene defect and reducing disease progression. Findings from this study will inform the rational design of
therapeutics for Huntington's disease and other neurological disorders associated with faulty mismatch repair.

application_id: 11175250
activity_code: F30
title: Characterization of the role of ZSWIM8 and target-directed microRNA degradation in hematopoiesis
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
MicroRNAs are small, noncoding RNAs that regulate the expression of more than half of human protein-coding
genes and are crucial for proper production of blood and immune cells. MicroRNA levels can be tuned through
a process called target-directed microRNA degradation, however, the role of this microRNA degradation in
hematopoiesis (the generation of blood cell lineages from a hematopoietic stem cell) is unknown. This proposal
will establish foundational knowledge about the role of this degradation process in mammalian hematopoiesis,
with a focus on platelet-producing cells, which will identify potential therapeutic targets for platelet-related blood
clotting conditions.

application_id: 11066534
activity_code: K43
title: Insulin-like Growth Factor-1 (IGF-1) signalling in immunometabolism of TB and TB-Diabetes comorbidity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CAPE TOWN
phr: Project Narrative
Diabetes is the second leading cause of deaths after TB in South Africa and increases the risk of developing TB.
The functions of IGF-1 in metabolism, genetics, and immunity in TB and TB/T2DM has not been studies
comprehensively. Using advanced training in immunometabolism and immunogenetics this project will define
the associations of IGF-1/IGF-1R in patients and assess an IGF-1R inhibitor as adjunctive therapy in vivo, to
enhance our understanding of IGF-1 signaling in TB, T2DM and TB/T2DM comorbidity.

application_id: 11336948
activity_code: K23
title: Dissecting the Ketone Metabolic Axis in Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Patients who have heart failure, or the inability of the heart to pump blood efficiently to the body, have reduced
quality of life, exercise intolerance, and are at greater risk for poor outcomes including death. This proposal
seeks to define a novel pathway of treatment in patients with heart failure using an alternative energy source
(ketone bodies). If beneficial, the results of our study may inspire larger studies and trials of such therapy in
patients with heart failure in order to improve symptoms, quality of life, and ultimately reduce morbidity and
mortality from heart failure.

application_id: 11076181
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular analysis of radial glial-like cells and their roles in nervous system regeneration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PUERTO RICO RIO PIEDRAS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE. Radial glia-like cells (RGLC) remain an untapped potential therapy source in response to central nervous system injury. This proposal aims to establish an evolutionary relationship between vertebrate and echinoderm RGLC, increment RGLC marker repertoire, and identify transcription factors providing de-differentiation capacities to the RGLC population. This study will provide a fundamental understanding of this cell population, its subtypes in the regenerating nervous tissue, and the critical gene regulators needed to establish central nervous tissue therapy in adults.

title: Elucidating the Neurocircuitry of Irritability with Ultra-High-Field Neuroimaging to Identify Novel Therapeutic Targets
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Poorly understood biological mechanisms of irritability – a feature of depression in 40-50% of adults with
major depressive disorder (MDD) that is associated with poorer quality of life, more chronic course of illness,
and elevated suicidal ideation – have hindered our efforts to develop novel treatments. We propose to study
healthy individuals and adults with MDD in order to understand how irritability is associated with aberrant function
of key brain regions at the neurocircuit level, and to identify changes in neurocircuit function that are specific to
pharmacological treatment-related improvements in irritability. A better understanding of these neurocircuit
mechanisms will facilitate our development of targeted treatment strategies that will reduce the suffering of ...

application_id: 11036356
activity_code: K08
title: Longitudinal Associations of Posttraumatic Stress Disorder with Incident Type 2 Diabetes and Poor Glycemic Control in a Large National Cohort
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: Project Narrative The proposed project leverages a unique longitudinal cohort of over 6 million individuals to examine the extent to which posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD), a psychiatric disorder that can develop after trauma exposure, is associated with incident type 2 diabetes and poor glycemic control in individuals who developed type 2 diabetes during the study period. This study also will investigate the extent to which associations between PTSD and type 2 diabetes differ across sex, race, and ethnicity. This work has the potential to advance our knowledge about factors that contribute to type 2 diabetes risk and ultimately may have implications for tailoring diabetes-related prevention and intervention efforts for individuals with PTSD.

application_id: 11139478
activity_code: K08
title: Characterization of Altered Immunity in Patients with Inflammatory Arthritis Induced by Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Therapy
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite unprecedented clinical success in the treatment of advanced cancers, immune checkpoint inhibitors are
associated with immune-related adverse events. Understanding the mechanisms of immune-related adverse
events is critical to identify valid biomarkers to predict and assess these events and to establish the optimal
treatment strategy targeting autoimmunity while preserving antitumor immunity, both of which are triggered by
immune checkpoint inhibitors. This work seeks to determine the cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying
arthritis associated with immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy by utilizing patient samples.

application_id: 11141701
activity_code: K01
title: An Intervention to Reduce Cancer Associated Thrombosis Through Improved Prophylaxis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Recent studies show that if preventative dose anticoagulation is provided to outpatients with cancer at high risk
for clotting, it reduces the risk of cancer associated thrombosis, which is the leading non-cancer cause of death
among this population. While guidelines suggest offering this potentially lifesaving therapy to high-risk patients
with an acceptable bleeding risk, it is not routinely done. This study will identify strategies to improve the
appropriate use of preventative anticoagulation for patients with cancer in diverse settings and pilot test an
intervention to improve the quality of care patients with cancer receive.

application_id: 11078656
activity_code: F32
title: Prosody perception in cochlear implant listeners
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Vocal prosody inflections like voice pitch are essential for expressing agreement or disagreement, to correct, to
signal novel information, or to indicate between-the-lines meaning. Listeners who wear cochlear implants do not
perceive vocal prosody as well as listeners with normal hearing due to device limitations, and struggle with
communication in ways that cannot be verified using percent-correct scores for the words alone. The goal of the
proposed research is to examine CI listeners’ perception of prosody relative to normal hearing listeners and to
study what acoustic cues are perceived by each of these listener groups when determining which words are
emphasized in speech.

application_id: 11046561
activity_code: K08
title: Role of Lipid Metabolism in Hepatic Ischemia Reperfusion Injury in Steatotic Livers
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The number of people in need of a liver transplant far exceeds the number of suitable donor livers, yet every year a substantial proportion of donated livers are discarded because they contain too much fat. Livers that contain too much fat are more susceptible to transplant-related ischemia reperfusion injury (IRI) resulting in poorly functioning grafts, a process that is neither preventable nor easily reversed. The proposed research will offer key insights into how lipid metabolism and composition participate in hepatic IRI, with the goal of identifying novel therapeutic approaches to attenuate or prevent IRI, ultimately increasing organ availability and improving recipient outcomes.

activity_code: K99
title: Dissection of specialized metabolism in plants using multiplexed perturbation
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Plants are the origin of many FDA-approved drugs and, as food, deliver a daily dose of drug-like compounds into our bodies. This project establishes an efficient strategy to decode the genomes of dietary and medicinal plants by activating and identifying hundreds of different biochemical programs in multiplexed assays. This work will help illuminate what molecules a plant can synthesize, allowing us to (1) make informed dietary decisions, (2) engineer the metabolic content of our food, and (3) produce high-value medicinal compounds.

application_id: 11138588
activity_code: K01
title: Predictors of Momentary Smoking Lapse Among Adults Who Smoke Menthol Cigarettes 
org_name: UNIV OF ARKANSAS FOR MED SCIS
phr: Adults who smoke menthol cigarettes experience disproportionate tobacco-caused health burdens and have increased difficulty quitting smoking. Our novel examination of momentary predictors of smoking lapse will further our understanding of how to prevent smoking lapses and relapse among adults who smoke menthol cigarettes and try to quit smoking. These data can be used to inform high-impact, technology-supported observational studies and interventions aimed at mitigating differences in tobacco-related outcomes among adults who smoke menthol cigarettes.

application_id: 11058422
activity_code: F30
title: Defining the Pathogenesis of Del(7q) in Hematopoietic Stem Cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Loss of all or part of chromosome 7 is among the most common chromosomal abnormalities in high-risk myeloid
diseases, yet the precise genetic and mechanistic contributions of these deletions remain unknown. There are
many purported myeloid regulators and tumor suppressor genes on 7q, suggesting 7q may be a contiguous
gene syndrome region in which the loss of multiple neighboring genes contributes to disease. This proposal aims
to leverage CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing to model 7q deletions in vivo and identify combinatorial gene deficiencies
that drive myeloid disease, and define the mechanisms by which dosage imbalances contribute to disease
development.

title: Effects of Platelet Transfusions on Neonatal Chronic Lung Disease and Sepsis-Induced Mortality
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Platelets are small blood cells that participate in clot formation to stop bleeding and also in the response to
inflammation, although only their clot forming abilities are considered when administering platelet transfusions
to premature babies, who often receive platelet transfusions in hopes of decreasing their bleeding risk.
Premature babies are a vulnerable population with high morbidity and mortality due to multiple disease
processes, and a recent large study found that babies who received more platelet transfusions had a higher
mortality and risk of lung disease than babies who were transfused less. In this project I will investigate the
effects of platelet transfusions in models of neonatal infection and lung disease and the mechanisms through
which platelet transf...

application_id: 10784489
activity_code: K23
title: Electronically Delivered Home Rehabilitation to Improve Symptoms and Activity After Pulmonary Embolism
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Narrative The incidence of acute pulmonary embolism is high (~400,000 cases per year), and residual shortness of breath (Post-PE Syndrome), primarily driven by deconditioning and anxiety, is common. This study will examine whether a specific, daily set of exercise instructions (by text or email) for 8 weeks can decrease disability after hospital discharge for acute pulmonary embolism. By studying this cost-effective intervention, we will learn whether a home-based rehabilitation program could reduce disability, improve quality of life, and decrease healthcare utilization (costs).

application_id: 11177773
activity_code: K23
title: Rise-HP: Reimagining Interventions for Support and Education in Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis; A Stakeholder Engaged Intervention to Improve Health-Related Quality of Life
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative
Patients with Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis experience poor health-related quality of life without access to
interventions that target improvement in this outcome. The goal of this proposal is to develop and determine
the feasibility and acceptability of a stakeholder-engaged behavioral and educational intervention to improve
the health-related quality of life of patients with hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Findings from this innovative pilot
randomized trial will lay the groundwork for a future, multi-site, randomized trial testing the effectiveness of the
intervention developed here.

application_id: 11060866
activity_code: K01
title: Basolateral amygdala to ventral subiculum network plasticity in alcohol dependent male and female rats
org_name: WAKE FOREST UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative
Alcohol use disorder directly affects over 16 million individuals in the United States alone and presents a
tremendous burden on society. The neuronal circuits that drive the negative affective state associated with
alcohol dependence are only beginning to be understood. The present proposal will seek to gain a better
understanding of the role of the basolateral amygdala to ventral subiculum circuit in a rodent model of alcohol
dependence in an effort to identify novel neural substrates for the development of more effective treatments for
alcohol use disorder.

application_id: 11121056
activity_code: K01
title: The Neural Basis of Active Sensory Gain Suppression in the Somatosensory System
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Object sensing and manipulation require selective focus of tactile sensory signals from specific locations on the
hand. Here, we determine how selective attention implements mechanisms of gain suppression to filter out
irrelevant tactile information, and enhance sensory representations encoding attended cutaneous stimuli.
Results from this study will provide fundamental insights into how distracting sensory information is discarded in
brain, which may lead to a better understanding of the underlying circuits mediating tactile perceptual deficits in
Autism and Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder populations. Our studies will also have a direct translational
impact on Brain-Machine-Interface (BMI) solutions that are specific to neuroprosthetic applications.

application_id: 11059097
activity_code: K08
title: Molecular regulation of cell fate and progenitor function in the distal human respiratory airways
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The smallest airways in the human lung are called the respiratory airways and are a site of injury
in many chronic lung diseases including Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (COPD). We have
identified a new lung stem cell in this region that is altered in COPD, potentially affecting the lung's
ability to heal and repair. Understanding the mechanisms that regulate this cell type will help
identify new avenues for treatment in COPD.

application_id: 11056098
activity_code: K23
title: Technology-Enhanced Asthma Care in Children at Clinic and Home (TEACCCH) Study
org_name: LURIE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed research is relevant to population health because pediatric asthma is a common chronic condition that affects millions of U.S children, especially children from urban, low-income communities. Children with persistent and poorly-controlled asthma should be taking a daily preventive medication, like inhaled corticosteroids, to reduce their risk for breathing problems leading to frequent emergency department visits and hospitalizations; however, a wide gap exists between what is recommended and adherence to medications. This project investigates using user-centered design methods and implementation science research to enhance a digital outpatient asthma intervention to improve asthma symptoms and adherence to medications.

application_id: 11083617
activity_code: K01
title: Etiological Role of Parasitic Exposure and Familial Susceptibility in Nodding Syndrome-Related Epilepsies
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative
Despite the high burden of epilepsies in sub-Saharan Africa, many commonly occurring
epilepsies remain understudied and poorly characterized. This proposal focuses on improving
our understanding of the role of host genetics and its relationship to exposure to Onchocerca
volvulus, a commonly occurring parasite, in the manifestation of epilepsy. Advances from these
studies will provide mechanistic insights into epileptogenesis of acquired epilepsies and new
targets for developing preventive measures.

application_id: 11083566
activity_code: K08
title: Regulation of Vascular Calcification by Adventitial Endothelial Cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Vascular calcification is the pathological deposition of minerals in the walls of the blood vessels, leading to the
hardening and increased stiffness of arterial walls, and increased cardiovascular morbidity and mortality. In this
proposal, we aim to define the effects of endothelial cells from the adventitia, the outmost layer of the blood
vessels, on the development and progression of atherosclerotic calcification, and identify the unique molecular
signature of these adventitial endothelial cells. The completion of the proposed studies may pave the way to
develop novel therapeutic strategies to selectively target these endothelial cells, thereby attenuating
atherosclerotic calcification.

application_id: 11132881
activity_code: K23
title: teleABLE: Adapting a Behavioral Activation-Based Intervention to Reduce Post-Stroke Sedentary Behavior Using Telehealth
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Elevated levels of sedentary behavior are prominent and persistent among stroke survivors and associated
with poor cardiovascular and cardiometabolic health outcomes which elevate the risk for recurrent stroke.
Engaging key stakeholders to adapt a promising post-stroke sedentary behavior intervention for remote
delivery using telehealth will expand its potential reach. Further, findings from the pilot randomized controlled
trial will enable future efficacy trials to advance the development of interventions that can reduce sedentary
behavior to improve health outcomes after stroke.

application_id: 11093958
activity_code: K23
title: Function of RELMβ in oral tolerance breakdown in food allergy
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Food allergies are on the rise, and yet the care of food allergic patients is limited by the lack of accurate
biomarkers of disease development and resolution, as well as by the absence of curative treatments. The goal
of this project is to investigate the role of RELMb as a novel candidate biomarker of food allergy and to elucidate
the mechanisms by which it contributes to food allergy pathogenesis. This research will address an existing gap
in the knowledge of food allergy mechanisms and improve the daily management of patients with food allergies.

application_id: 11176345
activity_code: K23
title: Metagenomic surveillance of pathogen and antimicrobial resistance gene transmission in hospitalized neonates
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Healthcare-associated infections in neonatal intensive care units lead to significant morbidity and mortality, and
are often caused by antimicrobial resistant pathogens. This proposal will use metagenomic next-generation
sequencing in novel ways to track pathogen transmission, identify environmental reservoirs of infection, and
understand acquisition of antimicrobial resistant microbes in hospitalized neonates. Together this work leverages
innovative metagenomic sequencing tools and analyses to advance infection control approaches and curb the
spread of drug-resistant infections.

application_id: 11081737
activity_code: K25
title: Functional connectivity alterations among opioid users in treatment
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: NARRATIVE
Abuse of prescription opioids is a major health problem characterized by the persistence of opioid use despite
negative consequences. Although opioid abuse is related to altered dopamine release in the mesolimbic and
cortico-striatal system, the functional connectivity abnormalities within these systems in opioid users are not well
understood. This research will use a novel method, concurrent TMS-fMRI, to examine the functional connectivity
alterations between the mesolimbic and cortico-striatal system among opioid users and demonstrate the efficacy
of rTMS in the treatment of opioid use.

application_id: 11056174
activity_code: K24
title: Communication in the Hospital: Impact on Patients with Alzheimer's Disease and Other Causes of Cognitive Impairments and their Surrogate Decision Makers
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Of the 13 million US older adults admitted to the hospital each year, nearly half will require a surrogate
decision maker due to Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and other conditions that cause cognitive impairment. The
process of decision making and the experiences of the surrogates are therefore central to the care of older
adults. Findings from this research will improve the quality of medical decisions for older adults with AD and
other causes of cognitive impairment and will improve the well-being of family surrogates.

application_id: 11163430
activity_code: K00
title: High-throughput dissection of transcriptional regulation in kidney disease
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Transcriptional dysregulation is common characteristic of cells in disease states, but the combinatorial complexity with which non-coding elements and chromatin regulatory proteins interact to determine gene expression makes it difficult to determine how transcriptional dysregulation arises and how it can be therapeutically reversed. In the F99 phase, I improve high- throughput CRISPR methods to accurately link regulatory elements with genes and I develop high-throughput recruitment assays to systematically identify the domains within human chromatin regulators that repress gene expression. In the K00 phase, I propose to develop high- throughput approaches that dissect transcriptional dysregulation in kidney disease.

title: Effect of Neprilysin Inhibition on Myocardial Interstitial Fibrosis
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) affects 2.5 million Americans, is rising in prevalence, and
is associated with substantial morbidity and mortality. Myocardial interstitial fibrosis contributes to HFpEF, but
few tools are available to diagnose or treat it. This proposal will investigate blood markers for diagnosis of
myocardial fibrosis, and whether an approved heart failure drug, sacubitril/valsartan, reduces myocardial fibrosis.

application_id: 11271363
activity_code: K23
title: MicroRNA as Diagnostic and Prognostic Biomarkers in Sarcoidosis
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Sarcoidosis is systemic disease with a highly variable clinical presentation and disease course, and appears to
have an increasing mortality rate. There are currently no reliable biomarkers to diagnose or predict disease
severity/progression, which further complicates management. The aim of this work will be to use differentially
expressed microRNAs and clinical characteristics followed longitudinally to create biomarkers that can improve
clinical outcomes in individuals with sarcoidosis.

application_id: 11140347
activity_code: K08
title: Preclinical Validation of Personalized Molecular Assays for Measurable Residual Disease Monitoring in Pediatric AML
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) accounts for disproportionately high morbidity and mortality in the pediatric
population; one-third of patients will relapse, many despite having no detectable disease after therapy by all
clinically available assays. Our research will evaluate the predictive value for relapse of novel molecular
modalities for measurable residual disease (MRD) detection in retrospective and prospective cohorts of
pediatric AML patients. Through this work we will lay a foundation for more comprehensive MRD assessment
in pediatric AML that will ultimately enhance relapse prediction and early intervention.

application_id: 11172598
activity_code: F30
title: Targeting the Calcitonin-BMP1 Pathway in Atrial Cardiofibroblasts to Ameliorate Atrial Fibrillation
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
My studies will provide a more detailed understanding of a signaling pathway regulating atrial fibrosis in atrial
fibrillation development. In the long term, I aim to apply my findings as a candidate for a novel gene therapy
approach to reduce atrial fibrosis in atrial fibrillation. Since atrial fibrillation is an increasingly prevalent disease
with excessive atrial fibrosis being a common driver, my studies could enable a more targeted and definitive
treatment for atrial fibrillation that would improve the quantity and quality of life for millions of patients in the
United States.

application_id: 11051786
activity_code: K08
title: Microfibril-associated glycoproteins attenuating pulmonary fibrosis
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis is a deadly lung disease of unknown cause, characterized by progressive lung
tissue scarring and airflow limitation, with limited treatment options. Our proposed studies will investigate
matrix-associated glycoproteins (Magps), which are highly expressed during fibrosis and appear to play an
inhibitory role in the progression of the disease. We will evaluate potential mechanisms by which Magps may
attenuate fibrosis, and characterize the cell populations that produce Magps, to identify exploitable pathways
for IPF treatment.

title: Characterizing alcohol use disorder recovery-related exposures on Twitter: Content, cluster, and network analysis
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Social networking sites (SNSs) represent both a potential barrier to alcohol use disorder (AUD) help-seeking
behaviors among young adults, as well as a potentially innovative approach to facilitate AUD help-seeking
among this population; however, there is little comprehensive understanding of the nature and impact of
various exposures to AUD and AUD recovery-related SNS content. Pro-drinking exposures may contribute to
decreases in perceived need for treatment due to the glamorization of heavy drinking behaviors, while on the
other hand digital AUD recovery narratives emphasizing lived experience, strength, and hope may serve to
normalize experiences of addiction and facilitate AUD help-seeking behaviors. This research career
development award will provide the candidate w...

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project seeks to develop personalized models of aggression using data from ecological momentary surveys and passive sensors from smartphones and wearable devices. This approach to studying aggression is important because psychological models of aggression emphasize that it is dynamic and contextualized but aggression is rarely examined in daily life where these dynamics can be studied, hindering our ability to predict and intervene on aggressive urges and behavior in the contexts where they normally occur and are most consequential. application_id: 11091593
activity_code: K01
title: Walk this Way: Physical Activity and Walking Biomechanics Lead to Early Knee OA Symptoms and Ultrasound-Detected Structural Pathology after ACL Reconstruction
org_name: MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
For at least 1 in 3 young adults with an anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction, the injury and surgery are
pivotal life events that lead to chronic pain, diminished long-term quality of life, and an increased risk for
osteoarthritis. This study will determine how longitudinal changes in physical activity and walking biomechanics
throughout the first year after surgery relate to poor symptomatic or osteoarthritis-related structural outcomes
over the same period. This study is significant because it will provide foundational evidence for which patients
and which visits to target with existing evidence-based interventions to mitigate the risk of poor outcomes
following anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction.

activity_code: K23
title: Prediction of outcomes for deep brain stimulation using multimodal MRI
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Parkinson disease is the second most common degenerative disease of the brain, affecting over 6 million people worldwide, and a rapidly growing cause of disability. Deep brain stimulation (DBS) is effective for treating many of the most disabling symptoms of Parkinson disease, however predicting the improvement received from DBS remains challenging. Our study will improve the care of those with Parkinson disease by developing a robust method using MRI to predict those who will benefit most from DBS.

application_id: 11088142
activity_code: K01
title: The role of Fbxl22 in the regulation of skeletal muscle mass
org_name: OKLAHOMA MEDICAL RESEARCH FOUNDATION
phr: Project Narrative
The loss of skeletal muscle mass can occur through genetic or external factors such as disease, hospitalization
or aging; and affects the quality of life for millions of people. In this proposal, I will be studying a novel and
potentially important protein (F-box and leucine-rich repeat protein 22) involved in the development of skeletal
muscle atrophy. The approach taken in this proposal has great potential for understanding the role of E3 ubiquitin
ligases in the regulation of skeletal muscle mass and function and the findings may provide a basis for new
therapeutic strategies for the treatment of muscle atrophy.

application_id: 11083767
activity_code: F32
title: Understanding how neuronal glucose metabolism changes in AD due to ApoE4
org_name: J. DAVID GLADSTONE INSTITUTES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s Disease progression is characterized by cellular expression of ApoE4, reduced metabolism in the
central nervous system, and protein pathologies involving Tau and Amyloid-Beta. The roles of neuronal glucose
metabolism and neuronal ApoE4 in Alzheimer’s Disease development are not well understood due to both the
complex metabolic interactions between neurons and glia. We will therefore clarify the role of neuronal
metabolism in the progression of Alzheimer’s Disease through a combination of genomics, metabolomics, and
targeted validation of these changes through quantification of protein-level changes and intracellular ATP levels,
enabling us to capture a complete picture of how neurons metabolically respond to their expression of ApoE4.

activity_code: K08
title: Urban rat exposure and pediatric asthma
org_name: TUFTS UNIVERSITY BOSTON
phr: Project Narrative Rodent exposure can trigger asthma, a common disease of urban children. The present proposal will increase our understanding of how children are exposed to rats and the impact rat population dynamics may have on pediatric asthma severity. This research will open novel data-driven primary prevention strategies that can be tested to reduce pediatric exposure to rodents and the allergens they produce in and around the urban home and school environment.

application_id: 10935974
activity_code: F32
title: Neuropeptide Y1 Receptor-Expressing Neurons in the Lateral Parabrachial Nucleus in Neuropathic Pain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative
Neuropathic pain is a debilitating form of chronic pain afflicting millions of patients worldwide but is poorly
responsive to current analgesic therapies. Recent preclinical behavioral data implicates the parabrachial nucleus
of the brainstem in the maintenance of both the behavioral reflexive and affective components of neuropathic
pain. This study uses in vivo and awake moving fiber photometry and in situ fluorescent hybridization, cell type
specific chemogenetics, and anatomical tracing combined with efferent terminal chemogenetic inhibition to
ultimately determine if excitatory parabrachial neurons (those that contain the type 1 receptor for neuropeptide
Y) contribute to the behavioral manifestations of neuropathic pain.

application_id: 11363029
activity_code: K23
title: Optimizing Deceased Donor Kidney Allocation Using Decision Science
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Kidney transplantation provides the best survival and quality of life for patients with kidney failure, but there is a
shortage of kidneys available for transplantation in the United States. This organ shortage necessitates the use
of some deceased donor kidneys with less-than-ideal characteristics, and the safe use of these kidneys requires
accurate and reliable assessments of organ quality; however, the current presentation of clinical data leads to
suboptimal decision making that discourages the use of transplantable marginal kidneys. This project aims to
use decision making science to improve the way that this data is presented, starting with deceased donor kidney
biopsy results, in order to improve the utilization of kidneys from the expanded donor and organ pool.

application_id: 11218675
activity_code: F32
title: Uncertainty and Expectation Formation in Acute Respiratory Failure Survivors
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Many survivors of acute respiratory failure (ARF) are left with disabling long-term sequelae of their critical illness,
leading to reduced quality of life (QoL). Interventions in the post-intensive care unit (ICU) setting have struggled
to improve QoL and there is an urgent need to understand relevant modifiable factors. The goal of this proposal
is to elucidate key factors associated with the development of two modifiable contributors to QoL, bothersome
uncertainty and health expectations, as these factors are potential targets for novel interventions.

activity_code: K23
title: Applications of artificial intelligence to the diagnostic evaluation of infectious keratitis
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This K23 application aims to develop and validate artificial intelligence tools to aid in determining the microbiologic etiology of infectious keratitis, which is a major cause of blindness worldwide. We intend to develop two artificial intelligence systems, one to determine the cause of infectious keratitis from clinical photographs and the other to read microbiologic smears in an automated fashion. These systems will allow more rapid initiation of directed antimicrobial therapy, particularly in medically underserved areas, and improve visual outcomes in infectious keratitis.

application_id: 11003748
activity_code: F30
title: The role of the DNA damage response in the development and therapeutic sensitivity of malignant testicular germ cell tumors.
org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative This proposal will elucidate the DNA damage response properties of testicular germ cell tumors (TGCTs) and the cells from which they arise, which will inform the mechanisms underlying their remarkable sensitivity to conventional genotoxic chemotherapy. Understanding the basis of TGCT chemosensitivity will apply broadly to the development of treatments for the many other cancers that do not respond favorably to chemotherapy. Additionally, investigating the malignant transformation of pluripotent cells will be important for improving stem- cell based therapies, which carry the risk of tumorgenicity.

application_id: 11088256
activity_code: K08
title: Somatic mutations in autism spectrum disorder
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder that affects 1 in 36 children in the United States. Somatic variants, variants that arise after fertilization, have been implicated in ASD risk, but the types of variants that are enriched in ASD probands as well as the potential contribution of somatic variants in non- coding gene regulatory regions are largely unknown. This proposal will apply innovative approaches to test the hypothesis that somatic variants in both gene coding and gene regulatory regions contribute to ASD risk and can reveal new genes and mechanisms involved in ASD.

title: Evaluation of Mitochondrial Cardiolipin Modification in Neonatal Hypoxia/Ischemia Encephalopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Current studies suggest that mitochondrial lipids play a key role in neonatal ischemia/reperfusion (I/R) injury through regulation of programmed cell death. The proposed research aims to investigate the role of cardiolipin, a mitochondrial phospholipid, following I/R injury using advanced cyclic ion mobility mass spectrometry in addition to several transgenic mouse-lines. These experiments aim to build a better understanding of neonatal I/R injury, uncover novel molecular mechanisms, and reveal potential therapeutic targets within mitochondrial lipids.

The proposed study addresses a key NHLBI priority, improving the quality of life for people with sickle cell

sickle cell screening and violence prevention intervention in the Sickle Cell Belt in Central India. The

title: The distributed basis of visual cortical variability
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Processing visual information is a key nervous system function for many animals. However, understanding the
neural computations that underlie visual processing has been difficult due to large variability in these circuits.
The aim of this proposal is to leverage modern electrophysiological and computational approaches to map and
probe the distributed brain network underlying visual processing.

activity_code: F31
title: The Novel Role of Cilia in Astrocyte Sevelopment
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Astrocytes serve critical roles in the function of the brain, such that disruptions to astrocytes underlie several neurodevelopmental disorders, making it crucial to improve our understanding of the signals that direct astrocyte development. This proposal will investigate primary cilia, signaling antennae present in almost every cell with critical roles in development, to determine how they regulate different stages of astrocyte development. This work will inform novel functions of astrocyte cilia and advance our fundamental understanding of ciliary signaling in developing astrocytes.

activity_code: F31
title: Deleted in malignant brain tumors 1 (DMBT1) expression and impact on colonic dysplasia
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Deleted in Malignant Brain Tumors 1 (DMBT1) is a large, secreted glycoprotein that may represent a critical link
between inflammation, infection, and colorectal cancer (CRC). The proposed research will investigate the
mechanism of DMBT1 loss in colorectal dysplastic transformation and determine if DMBT1 loss is advantageous
for CRC progression through changes in epithelial cell survival, proliferation, differentiation, and migration
potential. Understanding the molecular relationships between immune barrier function and the regulation of
epithelial homeostasis will ultimately improve CRC biomarker discovery and precancer diagnostics.

application_id: 11177049
activity_code: K23
title: Estrogen's Neuroprotective Effects on the Brain-Barrier in Restrictive Eating Disorders
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Estrogen deficiency is common in R-EDs, which may drive neuroinflammation given the anti-inflammatory
effects of estrogen on the brain barrier. This study will use a randomized controlled trial of physiological
estrogen to study the impact of estrogen deficiency (and replacement) on the brain barrier and inflammation in
R-EDs. The resulting data will greatly increase our understanding of neuroinflammation in R-EDs and facilitate
development of novel therapeutic targets.

activity_code: K00
title: Exploring the mechanisms of dysfunctional mitochondrial quality control in cerebrovascular disease and the aging brain
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Disruption of mitochondrial dynamics and quality control are key components of leading causes of disability, such as cerebrovascular disease and age-related neurodegeneration. The proposed research aims to investigate the contributions of dysfunctional mitochondrial quality control to cerebral ischemia/reperfusion injury, as well as the onset and progression of neurodegenerative disease. Utilizing innovate molecular and computational techniques, these studies will advance our understanding of mitochondrial dysfunction and inform the development of future therapeutics.

application_id: 11144548
activity_code: F31
title: Resolving the role of VTA dopamine and GABA neurons in associative learning
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Behavior often takes place in situations of motivational conflict, where opposing goals (i.e., consuming food
and avoiding threats) occur simultaneously, requiring the appetitive and aversive elements to be weighed and
integrated to guide choices. This proposal will use novel viral and imaging tools to dissect the role of dopamine
and GABA neurons in the ventral tegmental area, a brain region critical for motivated behavior, in
valence-based learning and decision making.

activity_code: P20
title: BCM-TSU CURED Cancer Research Education Program (C-REP)
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: CURED C-REP Project Narrative 
The CURED C-REP will provide an 8-weeks mentored cancer research experience that integrates Baylor College of Medicine (BCM) and Texas Southern University (TSU) infrastructures every summer followed by year- long outreach education activities that will increase the awareness, knowledge, and understanding of research, concepts, processes, and careers in cancer research/cancer health outcomes in a cohort of 20 TSU students. The proposed CURED C-REP will enhance cancer research education capacity at TSU, enhance workforce development, and improve BCM’s effectiveness in developing and sustaining research programs focused on cancer health outcomes.

title: Transcriptional reprogramming of drug-associated memories in the nucleus accumbens
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative
Substance use disorder (SUD) is characterize by recurrent drug-taking behavior that often resembles the renewal-induced relapse as the subjects are re-exposed to the original drug context, after a successful extinction-based treatment. Unfortunately, conventional extinction therapy and its adjunct medications have shown limited success in the clinic, perhaps due to a lack of understanding of the molecular mechanisms governing the transfer of the learned extinction memories from clinical to drug-associated contexts. title: Longitudinal microbiome-host interactions and clinical outcomes in drug-resistant tuberculosis patients
org_name: STELLENBOSCH UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Host microbial communities (microbiota) profoundly affect our health and are changed by antibiotics. However,
microbial disturbances during treatment for drug-resistant tuberculosis (a leading cause of death for which tens
of millions of antibiotics doses are prescribed annually) remain uncharacterized and may help explain clinical
outcomes and long-term overall health, including post-treatment sequalae. Through leveraging of an already-
funded observational cohort of drug-resistant TB patients on treatment, we will evaluate microbiota changes
before, during, and after treatment (including how these changes correspond to drug absorption), how specific
taxa correspond to microbial metabolic end-products, and the microbiota’s association with treatment response.

application_id: 10890615
activity_code: K01
title: Unraveling the role of osteocytes in metabolic dysfunction associated with obesity
org_name: UNIV OF ARKANSAS FOR MED SCIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Obesity-induced metabolic deregulation is linked to increased bone fragility. However, bone is also a crucial driver of energy metabolism, yet the impact of obesity on the bone’s metabolic function remains unclear. Here, we test the possibility that obesity disrupts osteocyte-intrinsic energy metabolism that in turn contributes to deregulation of systemic energy balance. Testing this hypothesis will advance our understanding of the cellular and molecular mechanisms that mediate the crosstalk between bone and metabolic tissues, which will improve the success of clinical efforts to reestablish energy balance while protecting musculoskeletal health in obesity. Project Narrative

application_id: 11223298
activity_code: F30
title: Nox4 and Epigenetics in chronically enhanced cardiac protein O-GlcNAcylation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Heart failure is a widespread and severe clinical outcome of poorly treated diabetes mellitus, and preliminary
work has defined an association between diabetic heart disease, DNA methylation, and protein O-
GlcNAcylation—(molecular marks implicated in the regulation of gene expression and protein function).
However, while the possible downstream effector, Nox4, is upregulated upon long-term increased cardiac protein
O-GlcNAc, the regulatory mechanism of Nox4 expression is understudied. This proposal aims to determine Nox4
regulation by DNA methylation, and Nox4’s necessity in the observed cardiac dysfunction during chronic
enhanced O-GlcNAc.

application_id: 11051866
activity_code: K08
title: Developing a Donor-Candidate Risk Prediction System to Optimize Lung Allocation and Transplant Outcomes
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: Project Narrative
The donor lung supply is inadequate to meet the ever-increasing need of patients awaiting transplant.
Compounding this problem, the US lung transplant system does not consider all known donor and patient
information available at time of an organ offer, missing the opportunity to maximize best use of organs and
survival gained from each transplant. This research will contribute to the development of a system that will use
all known data at the time of an organ offer to find the best donor and patient match improving utilization of
available organs and maximizing transplant patient survival.

application_id: 11054546
activity_code: F32
title: Photoelectrocatalytic Trifluoromethylation of Arenes via Trifluoroacetate Decarboxylation
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Trifluoromethyl groups are common in small-molecule therapeutics, due to the dramatic improvement in
pharmacokinetic properties caused by the presence of C–F bonds. A significant limitation of established methods
for the incorporation of trifluoromethyl groups into organic molecules is the high cost of many commonly
employed trifluoromethylation reagents, which hinders the discovery and synthesis of new trifluoromethylated
pharmaceuticals. This proposal describes the trifluoromethylation of organic compounds using a silver(II) catalyst
together with inexpensive, abundant trifluoroacetate as the trifluoromethylation reagent.

application_id: 11121031
activity_code: K08
title: Engineering Immunity for Broad Protection Against Betacoronaviruses
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Betacoronaviruses, including SARS-CoV-2, SARS-CoV and pre-emergent bat coronaviruses, pose a significant
threat to human health and broad countermeasures to prevent and treat these viruses are urgently needed. The
spike subunit 2 (S2) of betacoronaviruses is highly conserved, making it an attractive target for broadly acting
therapeutics. This proposal will generate new knowledge about the function of S2-directed antibodies and inform
novel approaches to vaccine development and antibody therapeutics for betacoronaviruses.

application_id: 11136885
activity_code: K08
title: Characterizing the Impact of Fragmented Care in Acute Respiratory Failure
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Survival from respiratory failure varies widely across hospitals, suggesting that improvements to the
organization and delivery of care to respiratory failure patients may prevent deaths. Yet, recent changes
affecting inpatient care have encouraged fragmentation—care that is spread out across multiple physicians
during a hospitalization. The overarching goal of this proposal is to understand the impact of fragmented care
on adults hospitalized with respiratory failure while identifying specific targets for protecting patients exposed to
fragmented care from harm.

application_id: 10887314
activity_code: K08
title: Identification of splice variant derived neo-antigens in head and neck squamous cell carcinoma as targets for tumor vaccine therapy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Narrative The proposed research is relevant to the NIH mission to seek fundamental understanding of mechanisms driving head and neck cancer to enhance health, lengthen life and reduce illness in these patients. Immunotherapy is a new method of cancer treatment that recruits the immune system to help target and kill cancer cells, and it was recently FDA approved for patients with recurrent or metastatic head and neck cancer, however less than 20% of these patients will respond to treatment. To improve response rates, we propose to identify novel neoantigens through interrogation of splicing alterations in oral cancer that represent potential vaccine targets to boost the immune system and enhance response to immunotherapy.

application_id: 11132961
activity_code: K08
title: Regulation of signaling pathways mediating epithelial cell injury in the neonatal intestine
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC) is a severe and often fatal intestinal disease of prematurity that
culminates in irreversible intestinal epithelial cell injury. Effective treatments for this devastating disease are
lacking due to an inadequate understanding of protective and pathogenic signaling pathways in the neonatal
intestine. This K08 career development proposal investigates the mechanisms involved in attenuating intestinal
epithelial cell injury in NEC, which is critical for designing new effective treatment strategies for this devastating
disease.

application_id: 11094757
activity_code: K99
title: Molecular determinants of synaptic specificity underlying a visuomotor transformation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative
Visuomotor transformation is a critical process linking ethologically relevant visual information with appropriate
behaviors, but little is known about its underlying circuit and molecular mechanisms. The objective of this
research program is to establish Drosophila as a powerful model system to investigate the molecular
determinants of circuit wiring underlying a visuomotor transformation using a combination of transcriptomics,
connectomics, and physiology. Molecular mechanisms of synaptic specificity uncovered in this work will be
applicable to brains of other animals, including mammals, and will provide a deeper understanding of human
neurological disorders associated with impaired visuomotor processing.

title: The role of ANGEL2 in TP53-dependent tumor suppression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Project Narrative RNA-binding proteins are emerging as principal modulators of every hallmark of cancer progression, however there is still little known about their cancer associated molecular functions and targets. ANGEL2, an RNA- binding protein, whose expression was found to be downregulated in multiple cancers, was found to modulate the expression of the tumor suppressor TP53. Consequently, this project aims to dissect the role of ANGEL2 in TP53-mediated tumor suppression, and to develop ANGEL2-derived peptides to enhance wild-type TP53 expression as a novel therapeutic approach.

application_id: 11146430
activity_code: F31
title: Mitochondrial Regulation of Interferon Response in Melanoma
org_name: SALK INSTITUTE FOR BIOLOGICAL STUDIES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Reduced interferon (IFN) signaling is major way tumor cells evade the immune system. Mitochondria are central players in cell metabolism, but their role in regulating anti-tumor immune responses is less well understood. This project examines the link between mitochondria and IFN signaling, which may provide new ways to target tumor metabolism to bolster anti-tumor immune responses and the effectiveness of immunotherapy.

application_id: 11101410
activity_code: K01
title: Machine Learning Approaches for Behavioral Phenotyping of Humanized Knock-in Models of Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: J. DAVID GLADSTONE INSTITUTES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Progress in Alzheimer’s disease (AD) research will benefit from new tools for detecting behavioral changes that
occur in early disease stages, when therapies are likely to be most effective at halting or reversing disease
progression. We propose to develop a new AI-based method for detecting subtle behavioral changes in late-
onset AD mouse models that lack clear behavioral impairments when tested with traditional behavioral
approaches. The proposed research will contribute a new urgently-needed tool for understanding and measuring
behavioral changes in AD and will increase the usefulness of mouse models of late-onset AD for the development
of new therapies aimed at improving behavioral and cognitive health in AD patients.

application_id: 11070198
activity_code: F31
title: Defining the functional and spatial properties of tumor-specific tumor-infiltrating CD8 T cells
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
Anti-tumor immune responses and response to immunotherapy rely on direct recognition by cytotoxic CD8 T
cells of antigens presented on the surface of tumor cells. Tumors like melanoma suppress native immune
response through induction of T cell dysfunction and exhaustion, but the direct interactions between tumor-
specific CD8 T cells and cells in the tumor leading to dysfunction are incompletely understood. Investigation of
the anti-tumor T cell specificities required for tumor control, and the intratumoral interactions of tumor-specific
CD8 T cells in melanoma, will contribute to a better understanding of the mechanisms driving anti-tumor T cell
responses and pave the way for future TCR-based therapeutic approaches.

application_id: 10914268
activity_code: K76
title: Incorporating Geriatric Constructs into Management of Inflammatory Bowel Diseases in Older Adults
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite >25% of Americans with inflammatory bowel diseases (IBD) being ≥65 years, <1% are included in clinical trials of IBD medications, resulting in a lack of robust data on safety and efficacy of these medications in older adults. Immunosuppression, associated with substantial risks and benefits that may be amplified at older ages, is the mainstay of modern IBD treatment; however, we have no tools to tailor treatments for older adults. This proposal seeks to study the impact of geriatric constructs of frailty and function as adverse event risk mediators after immunosuppressive treatments for IBD and generate a risk prediction model that can expand treatment options for the heterogeneous population of older adults with IBD.

application_id: 11105763
activity_code: F30
title: Boosting protein synthesis using antisense oligonucleotides to treat neurodegeneration
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Safe and effective gene upregulation approaches in the central nervous system have the potential to treat many
neurological diseases, including disorders of genetic haploinsufficiency and neurodegeneration. In the present
study, we introduce a novel strategy using antisense oligonucleotides to stimulate protein synthesis and evaluate
its applicability to ~40% of genes in the human genome, with a focus on proteins that could protect against
neurodegenerative disease. Our findings would introduce a new class of gene-boosting biologics that are widely
applicable, effective, and safely administered to the central nervous system.

activity_code: K01
title: Neurovascular Mechanisms of Kidney Disease Progression in Older Adults
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is highly prevalent and progresses faster in older adults, leading to a substantially higher risk for end-stage kidney disease, poor quality of life and mortality. This study will elucidate pathophysiologic mechanisms that lead to accelerated kidney function decline in older patients with CKD. The results of this study have the potential to identify new treatment targets to improve survival and quality of life in older adults with CKD.

application_id: 11179106
activity_code: F31
title: Characterizing structural variation at the amylase locus influencing oral health
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative In the United States, one in four adults has an active cavity, and of adults over 65, one in four has fewer than nine teeth. While oral health and the oral microbiome that supports it are both highly heritable, few genetic associations have been established. This project aims to better characterize genetic variation at the salivary amylase locus to identify any associations it has with oral health phenotypes as well as the prevalence of specific bacterial species in the oral microbiome that are known to impact tooth health.

application_id: 11056856
activity_code: K99
title: Hematopoietic stem cell defects in Diamond-Blackfan Anemia
org_name: ST. JUDE CHILDREN'S RESEARCH HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
I have used CRISPR/Cas9 technology to create a new human cell-based experimental model for Diamond–
Blackfan anemia (DBA), a rare but devastating blood disease that causes isolated anemia in children and global
suppression of blood formation in many older patients. Using my new approach, I showed that the mutations in
RPS19, the most commonly affected gene in DBA, impair the function of blood-repopulating hematopoietic stem
cells (HSCs). My planned project to study this impairment should lead to improved understanding of DBA, identify
new therapeutic strategies, and elucidate a new facet of ribosomal and HSC biology.

org_name: RESEARCH INST NATIONWIDE CHILDREN'S HOSP
phr: NARRATIVE The overarching aim of this study is to determine the mechanisms behind the highly successful treatment combination of trabectedin, an FDA-approved drug, and an antitumor virus against Ewing sarcoma and osteosarcoma tumors. application_id: 11146485
activity_code: DP2
title: Maternal immune activation remodeling of offspring glycosaminoglycan sulfation patterns during neurodevelopment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Maternal immune activation (MIA) is a primer for long-term neuropsychiatric consequences in offspring, although
the exact mechanisms underlying offspring neurodevelopmental defects resulting from these maternal immune
insults are unknown. Here, we provide evidence that MIA-induced changes in the developing offspring’s
extracellular chondroitin and dermatan sulfate glycosaminoglycan (CS/DS-GAG) sulfation patterning, which are
key regulator of axonal guidance and synaptogenesis during neurodevelopment, may result in developmental
defects that predispose the offspring to mental health disorders in the adult, and studies outlined within this
proposal aim to further investigate this possibility.

title: Assessment of a Radiomics-Based Computer-Aided Diagnosis Tool for Cancer Risk Stratification of Pulmonary Nodules
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pulmonary nodules (PNs) are detected via computed tomography (CT) in approximately 2 million adults in the
U.S. each year, but the current diagnostic process for PN evaluation and management results in delayed
cancer diagnoses and unnecessary invasive biopsy procedures among individuals with benign diagnoses.
Existing clinical malignancy risk prediction models provide only modest diagnostic accuracy, but radiomics-
based computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) tools, which synthesize quantitative features from CT imaging data to
predict cancer risk, may help clinicians estimate PN malignancy risk more accurately. This proposal will
evaluate the clinical utility, clinical effectiveness, and cost-effectiveness of a CAD-based risk stratification
strategy for PN evaluation and managem...

application_id: 11159557
activity_code: K08
title: Tracing the Evolution of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia to Richter's Syndrome: Defining Transformation
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative
Richter’s syndrome (RS) is an aggressive malignancy of B cells that arises in a subset of patients with the
otherwise typically indolent chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), and dramatically limits survival in CLL patients.
Despite advances in tumor sequencing techniques and analytic tools, the genetic basis of RS remains largely
uncharacterized. The objective of the proposed studies is to identify and trace the origins of RS arising from CLL
and define its molecular features in order to gain a deeper understanding of the subtypes of RS—which is
anticipated to improve both the diagnosis and therapy of RS.

application_id: 11109623
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanisms of adipocyte loss in mouse models of familial partial lipodystrophy 2
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Familial partial lipodystrophy 2 (FPLD2) is a rare disease that causes adipose tissue loss, dysfunction, and redistribution; FPLD2 is caused by mutations in LMNA, which encodes proteins important for nuclear function and gene expression. Mechanistic studies are required to understand how and why loss of LMNA function leads to FPLD2, and to reveal therapeutic targets for lipodystrophy patients. This project uses inducible adipocyte-specific Lmna knockout and other mouse models to identify mechanisms of adipocyte loss, which I hypothesize occurs because reduced lipogenic gene expression leads to an inability of adipocytes to maintain their lipid stores.

title: Charting the evolutionary development of novel genes and the molecular mechanisms of gland tissue organizationin cephalopods
org_name: HUNTER COLLEGE
phr: Narrative
The study of complex traits is fundamental to understanding the nature and behavior of living systems.
Most diseases, like Alzheimer or cancer have complex traits whose genetic characterization in model
systems have been essential to finding effective therapies. Similarly, venom is a complex trait that has
evolved throughout the tree of life, and studying its evolution has broad applications to understanding the
development of homologous tissues, the origins of novel genes, and the molecular mechanisms behind the
regulation and expression of bioactive compounds.

title: Characterizing the formation and maintenance of drug-biased beliefs in opioid use disorder
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite psychoeducational efforts about the potential consequences of drug use, relapse remains the modal outcome among treatment-engaged individuals with opioid use disorder (OUD).This clinical observation indicates a need for better understanding how undesirable information about drug use is evaluated and incorporated into patient’s beliefs about personal risk.
