application_id: 11308625
activity_code: F32
title: Defining Combined Pulmonary Fibrosis and Emphysema (CPFE) using Quantitative CT Imaging
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Combined pulmonary fibrosis and emphysema (CPFE) is a life-limiting, progressive lung disease with no
rigorously defined standardized diagnostic criteria, which limits multi-center and longitudinal studies. This work
begins to fill these critical gaps by establishing quantitative imaging features and thresholds to define CPFE and
assessing patient centered outcomes, including survival and lung function decline. By defining CPFE using
quantitative imaging, the broader goal of this research is to improve clinical care and early detection for this
devastating disease.

application_id: 11158844
activity_code: K01
title: Leveraging Large Language Models and Machine Learning Algorithms to Assess Depression and Anxiety Symptoms and Risks for Patients with Cardiovascular Disease or Diabetes Mellitus
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Depression and anxiety are common among patients with cardiovascular disease (CVD) or diabetes mellitus
(DM), having detrimental impact on these chronic conditions. This project will identify symptomatic issues, care
needs, and at-risk subgroups of depression and anxiety in CVD and DM patients by analyzing patient portal
messages and electronic health records using large language models and machine learning algorithms,
approaches that can overcome the current multilayer barriers to timely detection. The findings will contribute to
the preparation of targeted and tailored interventions, ultimately improving mental health outcomes.

activity_code: DP1
title: Deciphering the single-nucleus genomic regulatory structure of opioid use disorder in the human brain
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The goal of the proposed project is to better understand the molecular underpinnings and cell diversity of opioid
use disorder in the human brain. This study will conduct a joint profiling of methylomics, hydroxymethylomics,
3D chromatin structure, and transcriptomics of OUD in single nuclei from human postmortem brain to identify
molecular targets that may inform prevention and treatment efforts.

application_id: 11310457
activity_code: K01
title: Assessment of Early Life PFAS Exposure in Perinatal Biospecimens, Infant Formula, and Breastmilk
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Early-life exposure to per- and polyfluorinated alkyl substances has been linked to a number of adverse health effects; however, PFAS toxicity and exposure assessment studies largely focus on a small number of PFAS. This proposal will evaluate aggregate exposure to PFAS mixtures using untargeted, unbiased methodology in maternal serum, placenta, cord blood, infant serum, formula, and breastmilk. The information collected in this study can be used to guide selection of PFAS for future studies and to inform guidance on breastfeeding in PFAS exposed populations.

application_id: 11112429
activity_code: D43
title: Infection-Associated Cancer Research Training Program in Mali
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: NARRATIVE
In Mali, the 8th largest African country, a low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) located in West Africa,
infection-associated cancers (IACs) are increasing and represent more than a quarter (27%) of all cancers. Few
African scientists have the epidemiological, molecular pathology, biostatistics and bioinformatics training needed
to design and conduct population-based and mechanistic studies on IACs. This research training program will
capitalize on a decade of collaboration between Malian and US investigators to create capacity to develop and
implement new contextually appropriate and culturally adaptive strategies to reduce in incidence, morbidity, and
mortality of IAC in Mali and other LMICs in the region.

application_id: 11110349
activity_code: K08
title: SPOT-IT: Sepsis Prediction in Oncology Through Implementation Science and Technology
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Because hospitalized patients with cancer face unacceptable morbidity and mortality from sepsis (a life-
threatening dysregulated response to infection), there is urgent need for interventions to identify oncologic
sepsis early and enable timelier appropriate treatments. However, incumbent electronic health record-based
sepsis prediction tools – linked to improved mortality in other populations – have failed to deliver on their
promise in oncology because of design and implementation limitations. This project aims to advance the
simultaneous and iterative development of a machine learning tool to predict oncologic sepsis alongside the
user-centered design of implementation strategies to improve sepsis care and outcomes for hospitalized
patients with cancer.

application_id: 11112329
activity_code: K08
title: Cortico-hippocampal circuit dysfunction in an Scn1a mouse model of epilepsy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Epilepsy is a debilitating chronic disease that affects over 3 million people in the United States, many of whom
have ongoing seizures despite optimized treatments. I propose to determine the mechanism and consequences
of a dysfunctional neuronal circuit that I have identified in a genetic mouse model of epilepsy, using cutting-edge
techniques for the manipulation and recording of defined neurons within the brain. This mentored career
development award will fill gaps in my scientific and technical knowledge and skill set, preparing me for a career
devoted to development of novel, mechanistic, circuit-based therapies in preclinical experimental models of
epilepsy that can be translated to patient care.

application_id: 11095810
activity_code: F30
title: Characterizing the RNA-mediated recruitment of histone deacetylases to chromatin
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research project is aimed at elucidating the role of RNA and RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) in
regulating histone deacetylase (HDAC) recruitment to gene targets. This molecular understanding has the
potential to identify highly precise therapeutic targets for inhibition of HDACs in cancers where HDACs are
overrepresented. Thus, the proposed work is relevant to the NIH mission of health as it could lead to improved
cancer treatments with fewer side effects.

application_id: 11105963
activity_code: K01
title: Identifying barriers and facilitators of harm reduction among adult dual users of e-cigarettes and cigarettes
org_name: FLORIDA INTERNATIONAL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dual use of e-cigarettes and cigarettes is common among adults in the United States. Most dual users report
using e-cigarettes with the intention of quitting; however, these cigarette smokers are faced with contextual
barriers/facilitators to harm reduction as well as daily triggers and settings associated with cigarette smoking.
This study proposes identifying these barriers/facilitators and daily triggers and settings of cigarette smoking in
adult dual users, which will provide evidence to help research and clinical practice for effective intervention
methods to address barriers to harm reduction.

application_id: 10890644
activity_code: K23
title: Graft harvest as a model of tendon injury; Trajectory of recovery and clinical outcomes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There is a poor understanding of tendon healing in humans, which has led to a disconnect between the way treatments are designed for overuse tendon injuries and their use in clinical practice, placing patients at risk for long-lasting impairments, injury recurrence, impaired function, and decreased quality of life. Graft harvest (removing a portion of the tendon to use in another area of the body) provides an opportunity to improve our understanding of tendon healing and narrow the gap between research and clinical practice. Long-term, this will allow us to design better treatments and measures of treatment effectiveness, which will accelerate and enhance recovery from overuse tendon injuries.

title: Screening Mammography: Early Breast Cancer Detection for All at Risk
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: Sexual minority (SM) individuals are often diagnosed with late-stage cancer and often die from cancer prematurely. org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The overarching goal of this K01 Career Development Award is to foster Dr. Andrea Titus’s transition to

disease disparities. The proposed study will draw on multi-level statistical and machine learning approaches to
investigate associations between fine particulate matter (PM2.5) and disparities across a range of
cardiovascular disease outcomes, leveraging electronic health record data from a national cohort of U.S.

pollution-attributable cardiovascular outcomes and will shed light on potential population subgroups that may

application_id: 11141135
activity_code: K08
title: Defining the mechanism of inositol 1,4,5-triphosphate receptor-mediated metastatic liver colonization in colorectal cancer
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Metastasis is the overwhelming cause of death in solid organ tumors like colorectal cancer but remains poorly
understood at the molecular level. We have discovered a critical role for the type 3 inositol 1,4,5-triphosphate
receptor (ITPR3), a protein involved in controlling intracellular calcium signaling, in facilitating colorectal cancer
metastasis to the liver. The proposed research will define the mechanisms by which ITPR3 allows colorectal
cancer cells to overcome physiological barriers like extracellular matrix detachment to colonize distant tissues
and test its potential as a therapeutic target, leading to innovative strategies to block cancer recurrence or
progression.

application_id: 11160508
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of Glycosyl Ceramides in Heart Failure and Recovery
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Heart failure (HF) afflicts 6.5 million US adults and is associated with significant morbidity and mortality. Reverse
remodeling and recovery of cardiac function has been reported in ~15% of patients with advanced HF. Fatty acid
metabolism and more specifically sphingolipids play a pivotal role in the pathogenesis of HF. Understanding the
factors and mechanisms associating FA metabolism with cardiac recovery could lead to the development of
novel personalized therapies to improve outcomes in the greater HF population.

application_id: 11059182
activity_code: K08
title: Multi-omic Risk Prediction of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease in European- and African-Ancestry Populations
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Early identification of individuals at high risk for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) has been partially
addressed by genetic risk scores and gene expression prediction models, though these tools are suboptimal in
non-Europeans. We propose developing a multi-ancestry genetic and blood- and lung-based gene expression

application_id: 11115669
activity_code: K08
title: Clinical and biologic health trajectories after early life adversity
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Project Narrative
Child maltreatment is a significant cause of morbidity and mortality in the U.S. Better understanding of
proximate mental and physical health predictors after childhood traumatic stressful events (TSEs), including
genetic susceptibility and early biologic embedding by epigenetic age acceleration (EAA) and increased
inflammation, may facilitate targeted interventions to ameliorate the long-term health burden. Using the
Philadelphia Neurodevelopmental Cohort (PNC) and Center for Applied Genomics (CAG) pediatric
biorepositories at The Children’s Hospital of Philadelphia, this project will test the hypothesis that childhood
TSEs are associated with a spectrum of mental and physical health consequences that are influenced by
genetic susceptibility and biologic embedding.

activity_code: K23
title: Sex, Gender, and HIV Transmission: Defining the Impact of Biological Sex and Sex Hormones on Epithelial and Immune Cell Transcriptomics and HIV Transmission in Human Rectal Tissues
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Many transgender people have an increased risk of HIV acquisition compared to the general population, yet little is known about how the use of gender-affirming hormones may impact mucosal HIV transmission. In this proposal, we will examine the effects of biological sex and sex hormone treatment on rectal mucosal epithelial and immune cell transcriptomic diversity and function, and we will assess the impact of sex hormone treatment on rectal mucosal immune responses and HIV susceptibility. A better understanding of rectal HIV transmission among transgender people utilizing gender-affirming hormone therapy could contribute to the development of sex- and gender-specific biomedical HIV prevention interventions.

title: Infant arousal as a predictor of functional outcomes in Down syndrome (DS)
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Individuals with Down syndrome (DS) have heterogeneous developmental outcomes and varying support needs which impact quality of life and contribute to significant family stress and financial burden. Identification of objective early markers, with robust neurobiological underpinnings, of specific cognitive, social, and sensory outcomes is key for advancing and tailoring early interventions for DS. This series of testable experiments described in this proposal will yield physiological biometrics of arousal that are objective, present in infancy, and provide clinically actionable targets for treatment in early life to ultimately lead to improved functional outcomes for those with DS.

application_id: 11128573
activity_code: K01
title: Precision medicine approaches in the diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia in children
org_name: LURIE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Community-acquired pneumonia is one of the most common infections among children in the United States. However,
accurately diagnosing community-acquired pneumonia can be challenging due to its similarity with other common
respiratory infections, resulting in misuse of diagnostic tests and overuse of antibiotics. The proposed research will
develop a prediction model for community-acquired pneumonia using data from the electronic health record and design a
clinical decision support system that will improve use of radiographs and antibiotics, thus improving health outcomes of
children with this infection.

org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Hereditary spastic paraplegia (HSP) is the most common cause of inherited spasticity and associated
disability worldwide. Loss-of-function variants in the genes encoding the adaptor protein complex 4 (AP-4)

application_id: 11118911
activity_code: K23
title: Personalizing Antimicrobial Use in Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The current approach to treatment of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP)—a common, costly, and highly
morbid infection—is inflexible and impersonal. The present approach increases side-effect risk, promotes drug-
resistant infections, and under-treats many patients. This project aims to surmount barriers to personalizing
VAP antibiotic treatment durations by identifying lung biomarkers that reliably identify cure of infection and
creating novel VAP trial outcomes that inform bedside care.

application_id: 11161189
activity_code: K08
title: Consequences of combined prenatal alcohol exposure and acute placental ischemia on frontal cortical-sensitive behavior, structure, and physiology in juvenile offspring
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEW MEXICO HEALTH SCIS CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The co-occurrence of prenatal alcohol exposure and reduced placental blood flow during pregnancy is a
relatively common, yet largely unrecognized problem which can diminish a child’s ability to focus and learn later
in life. This research project will employ a novel preclinical model combining moderate prenatal alcohol exposure
and acutely decreased placental blood flow to investigate the adverse consequences of these combined prenatal
insults on frontal cortical brain structure and functions associated with cognition in juvenile offspring.

application_id: 11175957
activity_code: F30
title: Engineering B cells for enhanced HIV control
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE.
Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) remains a considerable burden in healthcare. We are developing a new
therapy that could enhance the ability of people living with HIV to control the virus by reprograming B cells to
secrete potent anti-HIV antibodies. This technology could also be applicable to a wide range of diseases,
including cancer, and other infectious diseases.

application_id: 11122243
activity_code: K23
title: Characterizing Sleep Signatures and its effects on Cognition in New-Onset Temporal Lobe Epilepsy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Project Narrative
In the United States, as many as 3 million individuals with epilepsy experience cognitive and sleep problems, in
addition to seizures, contributing to poor quality of life. This project will characterize the sleep architecture
patterns in epilepsy that are associated with memory and attention deficits. The long-term goal of this project is
to develop therapeutic interventions for sleep disorders and cognitive deficits in people with epilepsy.

application_id: 11098711
activity_code: K23
title: The Contribution of Late Onset Unexplained Epilepsy to Cognitive Decline and its Interaction with Vascular and Alzheimer's Disease Pathologies
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Epilepsy can be the harbinger of the aging brain and the third most common neurological disorder in the
elderly with up to half of new onset cases with no identifiable cause. This work seeks to understand the causes
and cognitive trajectory of late onset unexplained epilepsy (LOUE) by exploring the burden of Alzheimer’s
disease and cerebrovascular markers and whether epilepsy related factors worsen these pathologies and
cause accelerated cognitive decline. This area of research is especially relevant given the aging population
and the importance of identifying treatment targets that can prevent and treat LOUE, and the work’s findings
can be applied to diseases where seizures are a common comorbidity such as Alzheimer’s disease.

application_id: 11092292
activity_code: K01
title: Hippocampal memory mechanisms in nicotine relapse
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cigarette smoking and nicotine dependence are the major cause of preventable death in the United States. A
potent driver of relapse to smoking are places (e.g., bars or coffee shops) where the effects of nicotine were
previously experienced. The proposed research aims to understand the neural basis of these drug-place
associations by determining how the hippocampus and nicotinic receptors regulate learning in a mouse model,
findings that will potentially lead to novel treatments to prevent relapse.

application_id: 11161412
activity_code: K24
title: Discovery, Implementation and Mentorship in Personalized Cardiovascular Pharmacotherapy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Clinical, genetic and environmental factors that predict individual variability in response to cardiovascular
therapies can help identify patients who stand to benefit or be harmed by these drugs. However, the
integration of genetic predictors into treatment requires a paradigm shift based on assessment of their impact
on clinical outcomes, generation of evidence of benefit vs. risk (clinical utility) and value (cost-effectiveness)
and finally translation (implementation) into clinical practice. To facilitate this paradigm shift we propose to
incorporate genetic information with environmental and clinical predictors to help develop patient-focused
and population-based preventive and therapeutic guidelines for patients with cardiovascular disease.

application_id: 11248723
activity_code: F31
title: Determining the role of sperm microRNAs in early development and non-genetic inheritance
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
It is becoming increasingly accepted that gametes transfer non-genetic information from parent
to child that modulate inherited phenotypes. A species of non-coding RNA, microRNAs,
delivered from sperm to egg during fertilization have been demonstrated to causally induce
changes in early development ultimately leading to non-genetically altered adult phenotypes;
however, the molecular mechanisms underlying this phenomenon are unknown. In this project, I
will determine the initial functions of sperm microRNAs in regulating early development and
uncover the molecular mechanism underlying the paternal transfer of non-genetic information in
mammals.

application_id: 11036312
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanistic characterization of vaginal microbiome-metabolome associations and metabolite-mediated host inflammation
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative The vaginal microbiome is linked to important health conditions including bacterial vaginosis (BV), preterm birth, HIV, and cervical cancer, but reasons for these links are unclear and current BV-directed therapies do not work well. We will study how metabolites in vaginal fluid, including the potentially harmful molecules putrescine and imidazole propionate, are produced by the microbiome, how they cause inflammation in the vagina, and whether they can be blocked by pharmacological agents. The results will provide important new knowledge about causes of BV and vaginal diseases and will help to find new therapies for preventing and treating these diseases in women globally.

application_id: 11122202
activity_code: K23
title: Identification of Young Infants with Bronchiolitis at Low Risk of Developing Respiratory Progression
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Up to one-third of young infants aged ≤90 days old who present to the emergency department (ED) with viral
bronchiolitis have potentially avoidable hospitalizations, contributing to preventable exposure to hospital-
acquired infections, undue caregiver distress, and high healthcare costs. An accurate clinical prediction model
is needed to identify young infants at low risk of developing respiratory progression who can be safely
discharged to home. Using clinical history and physical examination factors, identification of the etiologic
viruses, and physiologic time series data measured from bedside monitors, the proposed study will create new
prediction models to guide clinical decisions and improve clinical outcomes for young infants with bronchiolitis.

title: Mechanisms and strategies to rescue suboptimal T cell priming in colon cancer
org_name: COLD SPRING HARBOR LABORATORY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite remarkable clinical success of immune checkpoint blockade in a range of cancers, most colorectal
cancer (CRC) patients do not respond to these treatments. Using powerful new mouse models of CRC and
cutting-edge tools in immunology, I propose to deeply investigate T cell dysfunction programmed by suboptimal
priming. This project aims to delineate mechanisms of efficacy and resistance to therapies targeting the T cell
priming axis, with the goal of guiding clinical investigation of novel combination immunotherapies for CRC.

The coordinated sequential presentation of angiogenic growth factors is critical for angiogenesis, but many

revascularized tissues. This project will apply a combination of computational design and drug delivery
approaches to establish affinity-based biomaterials as controlled release systems that mimic the coordinated
presentation of angiogenic proteins seen within a native context, providing a therapeutic approach for
reconstituting the angiogenic healing cascade. The ability to tune the release of multiple proteins will provide a
platform for systematically investigating how the temporal regulation of protein presentation mediates

application_id: 11249518
activity_code: F31
title: New biophysical and immunoregulatory mechanisms in neutrophil extracellular trap mediated lung dysfunction in cystic fibrosis
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) are being increasingly recognized as important contributors to the
pathogenesis of muco-obstructive airway diseases including asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
(COPD), and most prominently in cystic fibrosis (CF). This project will 1) evaluate new potential mechanisms of
NETs in altering the biophysical properties of mucus and 2) assess how airway mucus composition modulates
the formation of NETs. Successful completion of this study will identify novel mucosal drug targets to catalyze
the design of new therapeutics that reduce the impacts of NETs in CF and other chronic pulmonary diseases.

application_id: 10994165
activity_code: K23
title: Reward responsivity and depression in autism spectrum disorder: A multimethod approach
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Adolescents with Autism Spectrum Disorder experience depression at rates nearly twice that of their neurotypical peers, yet risk factors for depression in autism are not well understood and interventions are limited. Longitudinal, multimethod assessment of social and nonsocial reward responsivity and associations with depression symptoms in autism will advance mechanistic models, informing intervention development. This project will be the first to examine how relationships between reward responsivity and depression symptoms change over time in adolescents with autism, clarifying risk factors and potential intervention targets for this vulnerable population.

application_id: 10993191
activity_code: K22
title: Boosting IL-12-induced anti-glioblastoma activity via immunotherapeutic extracellular vesicles.
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Recurrent glioblastoma is associated with poor response to immunotherapy due in part to the immune- suppressive tumor microenvironment. Localized interleukin 12 (IL12) is making progress in therapy for malignant brain tumors by triggering anti-tumor immunity in patients. Our proposal will study IL12-induced anti- and pro-tumoral signaling in GB mouse models and develop a next-generation treatment whereby multi-factorial nanocarriers (extracellular vesicles) sustain IL12-triggered immunomodulating effects.

application_id: 11123430
activity_code: F32
title: Astrocyte-specific molecular cues guiding retinal angiogenesis
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Retinal vascularization in humans occurs in gestation, reaching completion at 36 weeks, and it is a crucial
developmental event involving precisely timed and localized events. Defects in any step of the process or halting
it before its full completion will lead to mispatterned or incomplete vascular coverage of the retina, which are
hallmarks observed in retinopathologies such as retinopathy of prematurity (ROP). Completion of this work will
elucidate unknown mechanisms of glial regulation of retinal angiogenesis, while also pinpointing important
molecular cues that may be involved in angiogenesis during pathogenic conditions.
